decisions.rationale:
Many runtime/surfaces/migration edits have wizard equivalents in the catalog. Before hand-editing, check:

  - New CQRS operation? -> praxis_register_operation (lands data_dictionary_objects + authority_object_registry + operation_catalog_registry rows atomically; import-resolves handler_ref + input_model_ref BEFORE writing; idempotent on operation_ref).
  - Add an optional field to an existing op input model? -> praxis_evolve_operation_field (plan-only wizard; returns a complete file-by-file edit checklist).
  - Provider/model onboarding? -> praxis_provider_onboard.
  - Field metadata / data-dictionary reproject? -> praxis_data_dictionary.
  - Soft-retire an op? -> praxis_retire_operation.

Hand-editing migrations to add catalog rows drifts from the wizard atomic helper, skips the import-resolve guard, and skips receipts. Hand-editing handler files where the wizard would also write the migration leaves the two out of sync. BUT - if you have checked the catalog and no wizard covers the change you need (novel runtime logic, new business rule, novel migration), the edit IS canonical.

Keywords that should trigger this check: add column, register operation, new operation, add field, evolve operation, CQRS handler, catalog INSERT.
decisions.title: Check catalog before manual edits — if no wizard exists, edit is canonical

decisions.rationale:
Default to autonomous. Anything that requires a human in the loop to function — approval gates, "Provide X" uncertainty cards, review-before-acceptance human checkpoints, operator sign-off requirements — is wrong-direction by default and must either be auto-resolved or removed unless the operator explicitly says it stays.

**Why:** Praxis is LLM-first infrastructure. The operator (LLM agent) drives the work; humans supervise via Moon / chat / roadmap, they don't unblock. A feature that gates on human approval makes the platform unusable for the LLM as primary user. The product thesis (LLM-first infrastructure powered by a trust compiler) requires that legal next actions be computable from typed state, not waiting on a human signal.

**How to apply:**
- When designing or extending any compose/launch/dispatch surface, check whether it requires human action to advance. If yes, redesign so typed state + autonomous resolution drives it forward. Examples to watch for:
  - `approved_by` parameters that block until set → auto-approve for trusted lanes; surface review as observability, not as a gate.
  - "Provide X" uncertainty cards that ask a human to fill missing inputs → emit typed_gap events the LLM can resolve.
  - Release gates that wait on operator sign-off → compute gate satisfaction from typed produces; auto-advance when matched.
  - Review phases that human-block → use auto/review LLM agents (autonomous), not human checkpoints.
  - "Awaiting approval" / "needs_review" lifecycle states without LLM resolution paths → add resolution rules.
- When auditing existing code, treat any "operator must..." comment or "human approval required" gate as a regression candidate worth questioning.
- Exceptions need explicit operator authorization. The user has said "for the most part, at least right now" — so some legitimate human-gates may remain (destructive shared-system actions, billing decisions, etc), but the default direction is autonomous and any new gate must justify itself against this directive.
- Pair with `feedback_credentials_per_sandbox_per_provider`: credentials minted at sandbox time per provider, not requiring per-launch human re-auth — same autonomy principle.
decisions.title: Default to autonomous. Anything that requires a human in the loop to function — approval gates, "Provide X" uncertainty

decisions.rationale: My shell has CLAUDECODE=1, so any `claude`-spawning praxis CLI subcommand fails on nested-session guard. mcp__praxis__* tools route via API server (clean env) → workers. .claude/settings.json now denies `Bash(praxis:*)`. Don't propose `praxis workflow tools call ...` from Bash for this agent. Codex/Gemini are unaffected.
decisions.title: Claude Code agent: MCP only, no bash praxis CLI

decisions.rationale: Agents default to the smallest working shape. If the work fits one Praxis workflow spec with depends_on, ship the spec — never a wrapper script. If the user asks to simplify, collapse further; never add a command, file, or layer. When Praxis primitives (DAG scheduler, praxis_wave, HeartbeatOrchestrator, CronScheduler, cron_scheduler) already handle the problem, use them instead of shell wrappers. Individual per-job specs are forbidden when one master spec with depends_on covers the same DAG. Violating this is treated as overbuild: delete the extra layer before shipping.
decisions.title: Agents collapse before orchestrating; use Praxis primitives

decisions.rationale:
Every change should actively reduce complexity, not just fix the immediate issue. Collapse multi-step patterns into single operations. Don't preserve historical layering when it can be flattened.

**Why:** User sees accumulated tech debt in the codebase (e.g., 5 sequential migration calls through separate async bridges when one would do). Incremental fixes that don't simplify are unsatisfying.

**How to apply:** Before implementing any fix, ask "can this be collapsed?" Look for: sequential calls that can be batched, redundant checks, historical layering that no longer serves a purpose, multi-file patterns that belong in one place. Prefer fewer moving parts over preserving existing structure.
decisions.title: Every change should actively reduce complexity, not just fix the immediate issue. Collapse multi-step patterns into sing

decisions.rationale: Before editing CQRS operation models, handlers, MCP wrappers, or operation catalog registration paths, run the CQRS Wizard surface first: praxis_operation_forge for operation path inspection and praxis_evolve_operation_field for existing input-shape changes. Manual edits are allowed only after the wizard says the operation already exists or returns the file-by-file evolution plan.
decisions.title: Use the CQRS Wizard before CQRS-shaped edits

decisions.rationale: Local Postgres + local API + local docker workers + local CLI LLMs = no real latency. If it didn't respond in 1-2s it's dead. No `until X; do sleep 5` loops, no waiting 30s for an LLM call. Just query directly.
decisions.title: Local stack: 1-2s polls, no 30s sleeps

decisions.rationale: If a workflow doesn't work end-to-end, don't build a babysitter cron + state file to manage it. Fix the underlying failure, verify with one fail-fast probe. Babysitters hide real bugs and burn LLM budget tracking dead run_ids.
decisions.title: Fix the flow, don't wrap broken flows in monitors

decisions.rationale:
**Updated 2026-04-26:** The earlier blanket "don't launch from Claude Code" rule was too strong. Corrected version below.

**Default to MCP for launches** — `mcp__praxis__praxis_workflow(action='run', spec_path=...)` works from Claude Code. Confirmed via `action='list'` returning the run history with no auth error. Use this lane for any workflow dispatch.

**Bash subshell still has the original limitation** — `praxis workflow run ...` via the Bash tool can fail when the SPEC spawns CLI agents (cursor-agent, codex, claude, gemini) as sub-agents because those subprocesses don't inherit the operator's interactive auth/session state. Specifically:
- `cursor-agent status` reports "Not logged in" inside Claude Code even though the operator's terminal is logged in.
- Spec B/C in the 2026-04-24 replay-ready run failed in ~40ms with `cli_adapter.exec_error` for exactly this reason.
- Recursion conflict: Anthropic's CLI → Praxis Engine → Anthropic CLI again creates session/auth conflicts.

**How to apply:**
- Workflow dispatch (any spec): use MCP first. `mcp__praxis__praxis_workflow(action='run', spec_path='artifacts/workflow/foo.queue.json')`.
- If a bash launch is unavoidable AND the spec doesn't spawn CLI agents (pure SQL / pure Python / pure HTTP), bash should work.
- If the spec spawns cursor-agent / codex / claude / gemini as worker CLIs, prefer MCP. If MCP is also unavailable, hand the launch command to the operator.
- Read-only Praxis surfaces (`praxis workflow query`, `bugs list`, `run-status`, `health`, `discover`, `recall`, `tools describe`) are fine via either bash or MCP.

**The point Nate made (2026-04-26):** "if you cant do it via api, or MCP, or Praxis CLI, im not sure what we built." The platform exists to enable programmatic dispatch — defaulting to "I can't launch from here" without checking is wrong. Always test the MCP lane before claiming a launch is impossible.
decisions.title: **Updated 2026-04-26:** The earlier blanket "don't launch from Claude Code" rule was too strong. Corrected version below

decisions.rationale:
Never leave monkey patches or shims in production code. Fix the root cause instead of adding workaround layers.

**Why:** Shims accumulate as tech debt and obscure the real architecture.

**How to apply:** When a test or integration breaks after a refactor, fix the test's patch targets or the module's import structure — don't add compatibility re-exports or wrapper functions to production code.
decisions.title: Never leave monkey patches or shims in production code. Fix the root cause instead of adding workaround layers.

decisions.rationale:
When the user asks for roadmap items "in detail" or "the gold," do NOT commit a sprawl of items first and call out sprawl in the risks section. Give the CTO build/no-build/defer/retire call FOR EACH ITEM up front, then file only what should actually be on the active road. Move speculative items to `operator_ideas` (pre-commitment notes), not the roadmap.

**Why:** 2026-04-26 thesis-sharpening conversation — filed 11 strategic items linked to a new architecture-policy decision, then noted "11 is sprawl" only as a closing risk. Operator pushed back: "But you didn't tell me if we SHOULD be building these." The judgment layer is the entire point of CTO-hat work; bulk-filing without ranking is the opposite of collapse-and-simplify.

**How to apply:**
- Before the first `praxis_operator_write commit`, output the ranked sequence: BUILD NOW / DEFER / RETIRE-or-HOLD with one-line reasons each.
- Identify the blocker(s) — items that gate everything downstream (e.g., wedge buyer decisions, falsification tests).
- Default to fewer items on the active road. Speculative or condition-on-success items belong in `operator_ideas`.
- A working roadmap should look like ~3 actively-claimed + a small backlog of explicitly-blocked items, not 10+ siblings under one decision.
- If filed-then-ranked happens anyway, propose retirements explicitly and wait for the call — don't autonomously retire commitments.
decisions.title: When the user asks for roadmap items "in detail" or "the gold," do NOT commit a sprawl of items first and call out spraw

decisions.rationale:
When Nate lifts a standing order, supersede only the exact `decision.YYYY-MM-DD.slug` he named (or quoted). Do not bundle related-looking decisions into one supersession.

**Why:** Standing orders in Praxis.db are durable production policy. Conflating two related decisions into one DB-backed supersession is hard to roll back and changes future-session behavior in ways the user did not authorize. Concrete incident 2026-04-25: user lifted `openrouter-primary-chat-engine` exclusivity (quoted that exact phrase). I auto-bundled `anthropic-cli-only-restored` into the same supersession because the design implication seemed to flow naturally — user had to interrupt with a hard rollback. The Anthropic CLI-only order was a separate decision he had not authorized lifting.

**How to apply:** When the user says "lift / remove / supersede X" and the design implication touches related standing orders, do NOT mutate the related ones. State the implication ("if we also wanted Y, we would need to lift Z — confirm?") and wait. Only the named decision gets the supersession write. This is true even in auto mode — auto mode does not authorize bundling policy mutations.
decisions.title: When Nate lifts a standing order, supersede only the exact `decision.YYYY-MM-DD.slug` he named (or quoted). Do not bundl

decisions.rationale:
When Nate reports a bug ("every provider I pick chooses openrouter"), treat it as a real backend bug and trace it from the wire. Do not first try to explain it away as a UX misunderstanding — even if the UX is also confusing.

**Why:** Today (2026-04-25) he reported that the operator console always created openrouter agents regardless of his dropdown selection. I responded with a UX-confusion theory (existing chips vs. dropdown only affects new agents). He correctly told me I was wrong and asked me to actually use the product end-to-end. Within one minute of doing that, the real bug surfaced: send_message read `PRAXIS_AGENT_CLI_PROVIDER` env var FIRST and overrode the per-agent provider stored on the session. He was right; I was making him do my job to convince me.

**How to apply:** First action on any bug report = reproduce by doing what the user says they did, end-to-end. UX confusion is a hypothesis only AFTER the wire-level trace shows correct behavior.

decisions.rationale:
In the Praxis repo, when a task needs an LLM to investigate, research, explore, root-cause, debate, or compile context, **dispatch it through Praxis Engine** so it runs on the operator's logged-in CLI agents (gemini, cursor-agent, codex, claude) via the cli_llm adapter. Do NOT:

- launch a Claude Code `Agent` tool call with `subagent_type: "Explore"` or `general-purpose`
- silently substitute hand-driven Grep/Read/Bash sweeps for what should be a dispatched LLM investigation
- treat investigation as out-of-band work that bypasses Praxis Engine

**Why:** Praxis is "LLM-first infrastructure powered by a trust compiler" — the operator's whole point is that work gets dispatched through Praxis to their logged-in agents, where receipts, route identity, evidence, and bug provenance accrue. Claude Code's own subagents bypass all of that authority. The operator flagged this with "using your own agents instead of ours" + "Tsk tsk" — they were chiding the substitution as a regression.

**How to apply:** When the impulse is "I should explore X" or "let me research Y":

1. First reach for Praxis knowledge surfaces: `praxis workflow discover "<behavior>"`, `praxis workflow recall "<topic>"`, `praxis_query`, `praxis workflow tools call praxis_research_workflow ...`.
2. If the work needs an LLM agent (open-ended investigation, multi-step reasoning), file a small Praxis workflow spec with `adapter_type: cli_llm` and an appropriate `agent` (e.g. `google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview`, `cursor_local/composer-2`, `openai/codex-...`, `anthropic/claude-...`) and `task_type` (`research`, `analysis`, `architecture`, etc.), then `praxis workflow run`.
3. The `praxis-research` skill dispatches deep, multi-angle research workflows for exactly this — use it for non-trivial questions.
4. Only use Bash/Grep/Read/Edit directly when **implementing** confirmed code edits, not for the exploration that precedes them.

**Boundary:** trivial single lookups (one file path, a known symbol's definition) can stay on Read/Grep — that's a tool call, not an agent dispatch. The rule kicks in when the impulse is "spawn an exploration session" or "let an LLM go figure it out."

**How to apply:**

- Never hand him a code snippet to edit — I make the edit.
- When blockers require human action, narrow to things only a human can do: credit card entry, email verification, physical hardware, account signups, billing support replies.
- Plans and instructions should describe *what I'll do*, not *what he needs to learn*.
- Explanations should be strategic/outcome-level, not code-level, unless he asks for internals.

decisions.rationale:
Valuable not overwhelming — both in tool output design and in explanations.

**Why:** An agent (or user) receiving 150 rows when only 27 are interesting is noise. Dense output burns context and buries the signal.

**How to apply:** When building agent-facing surfaces, lead with the enriched/interesting entries. Omit empty/default rows unless specifically requested. Same principle applies to explanations — say what changed and why, skip the inventory.
decisions.title: Valuable not overwhelming — both in tool output design and in explanations.

decisions.rationale:
2026-04-26: Repeated mistake in one session. Nate said "We are not using
openrouter, so im glad" — that was a *statement* about the Praxis platform
(openrouter routes are still in `task_type_routing`, but he doesn't want
to be using them). I interpreted it as "delete all openrouter rows from
the DB" and wrote migration 268. He pushed back: "Did I tell you to do
that?" I rolled it back. Then he said "I told you to stop fucking using
openrouter lol" — I interpreted *again* as "delete the rows" and rewrote
268. He clarified explicitly: **"I said I dont wnat you, you in this chat,
not praxis. You are not Praxis."**

The distinction:

- **Praxis** is the platform. Its DB rows (`task_type_routing`,
  `provider_model_candidates`, `provider_endpoint_bindings`, etc.) are
  operator authority. Modifying them needs an explicit "write migration
  that does Y" instruction with a concrete target and contract.
- **The chat assistant** (me) is a separate agent. Directives like
  "stop using X", "don't use Y" addressed at the assistant mean: stop
  bringing X/Y up in chat reasoning, stop suggesting it as a route, stop
  including it in summaries. They are NOT instructions to mutate the
  platform.

**How to apply:**

When the operator says "stop using X" / "I'm not using X" / "we don't
use X anymore" without naming a concrete write target:
1. Treat it as a chat-level directive. Stop mentioning X. Stop suggesting
   X. Stop including X in summaries.
2. Do NOT write a migration against the DB. Do NOT delete rows.
3. If the platform's behavior depends on X being absent (e.g., a rate-
   limit cascade routes through X), surface that as observation: "Heads
   up, the platform's task_type_routing has X at rank N — if you want it
   removed there, that's an operator_write." Do not act unilaterally.

When the operator says "write a migration that drops X from
task_type_routing" or "remove X from the catalog" — that IS an
authorization to mutate. The verb has to be explicit.

**Why this matters:** Praxis is the operator's system of record. Every
row in operator-authority tables represents a decision that lasted long
enough to be canonical. The chat assistant cannot tell which "stop using
X" comments warrant deletion vs. just chat-level avoidance. Default to
chat-level until the operator says otherwise. Twice in this session I
defaulted to deletion and burned trust.
decisions.title: 2026-04-26: Repeated mistake in one session. Nate said "We are not using

decisions.rationale: Copying Praxis skills into ~/.claude/skills creates sidecar drift: Claude may see a different skill set than Codex, Cursor, Gemini, workflow workers, or future runs. The durable authority should be a Praxis.db-backed agent skill registry with explicit skill identity, source path or content revision, target harness compatibility, enabled status, and queryable receipts. Filesystem exports can remain derived artifacts for harness compatibility, but they must not be the source of truth.
decisions.title: Agent skills belong in DB-backed authority, not per-harness sidecar copies

decisions.rationale: The praxis-debate skill is an in-conversation adversarial reasoning skill. It must use Praxis read surfaces for grounding, then run the debate inside the active agent response. It must not call praxis workflow debate, launch a workflow, or shell out to another model unless Nate explicitly asks for durable workflow execution.
decisions.title: Debate skill runs inline, not through workflow debate

decisions.rationale: Graph-lens UIs must not poll graph payload endpoints and diff snapshots to infer live state. The durable authority is committed workflow evidence in workflow_events/workflow_outbox; UI lenses subscribe to that stream, match emitted source ids against visible graph ids, then refresh the authoritative graph payload once per relevant event and mark changed nodes from the event source ids.
decisions.title: Atlas and Moon graph lenses use event streams for live changes

decisions.rationale: On macOS, Claude Code stores login state in the Keychain item `Claude Code-credentials` as JSON and noninteractive worker auth must use the extracted `claudeAiOauth.accessToken` bridged into containers as `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`. `~/.claude/.credentials.json` is not a reliable macOS authority path and read-only `.claude.json` mounts are forbidden for worker auth because current Claude builds still rewrite that file at runtime and can hang when the mount is immutable. Canonical operator recovery path is `scripts/praxis-up`, which rehydrates host-resolved auth into the control containers and re-runs the worker auth doctor.
decisions.title: Claude worker auth on macOS uses the Keychain token bridge, not mounted .claude.json

decisions.rationale:
When a sandbox is provisioned to run a job for a given provider, Praxis must mint or fetch a credential bound to that provider, scoped to that sandbox's lifetime, at sandbox-spawn time. Do not forward credentials from whatever shell or process happened to launch the workflow — the launch context's identity and the runtime's identity are different concerns.

**Why:** I tried to fix Anthropic-CLI-fails-in-workers by forwarding `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` from the host shell to the worker container (via `${CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN:-}` in docker-compose) and via Keychain fallback in `_cli_auth_env_forward`. Nate stopped me: that conflates the operator's interactive Claude Code session with Praxis's runtime identity, and OAuth tokens are session-bound so it's also fragile. The right model is per-provider, per-sandbox credential authority — same pattern OpenRouter+Keychain already follows.

**How to apply:** When sandbox provisioning needs a provider credential, resolve it from a Praxis-side credential authority (e.g. a `provider_session_credentials` projection seeded by an explicit operator onboarding command, refreshable, retractable, Keychain-backed). Inject into the sandbox env or a tmpfs-mounted file scoped to the sandbox's lifetime. Worker container env stays blank for credentials. Never propose forwarding `${CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN}` or any session-bound credential from the launch process. If Anthropic CLI is needed in workers, the credential authority must hold a refreshable Anthropic auth — not the operator's chat session token.
decisions.title: When a sandbox is provisioned to run a job for a given provider, Praxis must mint or fetch a credential bound to that pr

decisions.rationale: API key management must use macOS Keychain as the standard authority path. When a workflow, MCP wizard, provider onboarding flow, or LLM-operated setup surface detects a missing API key, it must not ask the LLM to receive or paste the secret and must not recommend plaintext .env storage. The remediation path is a host-side terminal/default-CLI secret capture prompt that reads the key with hidden input, writes it to Keychain under account=praxis and service=<ENV_VAR_NAME>, verifies resolution through the canonical resolver, and emits only redacted receipts/status. MCP and CLI remain sibling fronts over the gateway; the MCP wizard may request/launch the host capture flow, but secret capture itself runs on the host where Keychain is available. Containers and sandboxes receive only scoped hydrated env/file material from the authorized Keychain bridge at runtime.
decisions.title: API keys hydrate from macOS Keychain; missing keys are captured in a host terminal

decisions.rationale:
Store all API keys and secrets in macOS Keychain, not plaintext .env files.

**Why:** User explicitly requested secure secret management as standard practice. Plaintext .env files are a leak risk even when gitignored — any process on the machine can read them.

**How to apply:**
- When onboarding a new provider or integration, store the API key via `security add-generic-password -U -a praxis -s <ENV_VAR_NAME> -w <value>`
- Runtime resolution uses `adapters.keychain.resolve_secret()` which checks Keychain first, then falls back to env vars
- Never store API keys directly in .env, code, or migration files
- The Keychain service account is always "praxis" and the service name matches the env var name (e.g., DEEPSEEK_API_KEY)
decisions.title: Store all API keys and secrets in macOS Keychain, not plaintext .env files.

decisions.rationale: Provider auth = docker volume mount (host claude creds → /root/.claude/.credentials.json) + cli_auth_volume_flags chain. Sanctioned shell path = bin/praxis-compose-env (Keychain → docker compose env), nothing else. Never propose forwarding agent-session env vars into containers — violates per-sandbox per-provider creds standing order.
decisions.title: Auth via docker mounts, never agent shell env

decisions.rationale: When an MCP/LLM-operated wizard detects a missing API key, the intended user experience is an independent host-side credential entry window controlled by Praxis, not chat pasteback and not a raw command with a <your-key> placeholder. The window collects the secret in a focused private input box, writes it to macOS Keychain under account=praxis and the declared service/env-var name, verifies through the canonical credential resolver, then returns only redacted status to the wizard. Terminal/default CLI can remain the fallback transport when GUI presentation is unavailable, but the product-standard path is wizard-triggered secure host UI.
decisions.title: Credential wizards open a dedicated secure API-key entry window

decisions.rationale: Historical skills, queue specs, planning packets, artifacts, and hardcoded operator-local path references must be treated as evidence or derived compatibility material, not active authority. Cleanup should classify historical references before editing them, preserve immutable receipts where needed, and move live reusable definitions into DB-backed registry and data-dictionary surfaces with explicit stewardship, classification, lineage, and quality rules. Filesystem skill/reference exports may remain as derived harness adapters, but Praxis.db-backed registry and data dictionary rows are the source of truth.
decisions.title: Historical skills and references are not authority

decisions.rationale: Future agents should inspect praxis_ui_experience_graph before changing Praxis product UI so they can see surfaces, actions, routes, authority sources, source-file anchors, and known weak spots without launching Vite or guessing from React files first.
decisions.title: Read UI experience graph before product UI changes

decisions.rationale: Fresh-clone bootstrap validates database, API, queue, worker, and graph-runtime execution. It must not depend on LLM provider latency, sandbox tool availability, or model interpretation. Provider demos belong in examples, not the install gate.
decisions.title: Bootstrap smoke must be deterministic and provider-independent

decisions.rationale: When app functionality depends on external providers, model access, phone/SMS vendors, API-based agents, Copilot pickup, MCP connectors, or other configured execution authority, the app must not expose it as ready until setup is complete. Gated features should point users to an explicit setup wizard that explains options, required credentials, privacy/data-sharing tradeoffs, health checks, and fallback manual or MCP-driven paths. Third-party model routers such as OpenRouter and free models may be recommended as options, but the UI must clearly caveat that provider access can expose user data to that provider according to its policies.
decisions.title: Gated functionality must provide an explicit setup wizard

decisions.rationale: surfaces/cli/main.py declares _LEGACY_WORKFLOW_CLI_ALLOWLIST as the canonical set of subcommands permitted to delegate into legacy surfaces/cli/workflow_cli.py. Currently allowlisted: generate, validate, stream, chain-status. Test test_legacy_workflow_cli_delegation_is_allowlisted_and_bounded scans main.py source for legacy_workflow_cli.cmd_* references and fails if any are found outside the allowlist. Adding to the allowlist requires updating both the constant and the test — no silent drift. Partial mitigation for BUG-D92E6B38; full collapse of the four legacy command implementations into surfaces/cli/commands/workflow.py remains future work.
decisions.title: Modern workflow CLI delegates to legacy workflow_cli only via explicit allowlist

decisions.rationale: In a multi-machine Praxis cluster, machines used as cockpit/control-plane nodes must not claim workflow jobs by default. workflow-worker is an explicit execution-node profile; run it only on the host intended to perform local work. Shared Postgres remains the queue and authority plane, not the source checkout or execution host.
decisions.title: Cockpit startup must not imply worker execution

decisions.rationale: Compile preview is a query that recognizes messy prose, matches spans to authority and deterministic compile patterns, and returns suggested steps without mutation. Compile materialization is the command path that creates or updates draft workflow build state through canonical workflow mutation. MCP, CLI, API, and UI must use this shared front door instead of separate compile semantics.
decisions.title: Compile uses a shared CQRS front door across MCP, CLI, API, and UI

decisions.rationale: Compile truth lives in Postgres-backed compile-index and registry authority. Embeddings are an enrichment and ranking lane, not the compile backbone, so degraded semantic mode must leave structural compile behavior intact.
decisions.title: Compile authority stays DB-backed

decisions.rationale: Retrieval is the filter. If semantic retrieval against the catalog produces zero matches for operator prose, the compiler MUST emit exactly one typed_gap with reason_code=retrieval.no_match and an explicit apology label — NOT a hardcoded fallback list, template scaffold, or canned default shape. Keyword-gated constant functions that replace retrieval output with prose-independent content are information destruction: retrieval is prose-conditional and high-entropy, templates are 1-bit constants over keyword feature presence. build_graph projection MUST read exclusively from definition_graph.nodes where kind in {capability, narrative_block}, AND each capability node MUST carry its catalog-registered consumes/produces data_pill bindings (keyed to data_dictionary_entries rows), AND edges MUST be pill-typed (producer -> consumer on pill_ref) not generic derived_from_block strings. This is the one-graph-many-lenses contract: Moon renders the same typed graph the executor walks, the retrieval filter produced, and the data_dictionary authoritatively types. Parallel keyword filters writing to execution_setup.constraints.blocking_inputs are forbidden. typed_gap reason codes retrieval.no_match and retrieval.low_confidence are first-class compile signals, never silent defaults.
decisions.title: Retrieval is the filter — no template fallbacks in the compiler

decisions.rationale:
2026-04-26: When wiring up multi-step compile/plan-authoring, do NOT make the
synthesis call output a step-count that mirrors the user's prose verbs. That's
stupid — the user might say "build a custom integration" as one verb but
that's actually 6 build sub-packets + 2 fix sub-packets. Synthesis should
decompose by **work volume**, mathematically split across N forks, then
hand each fork to a parallel LLM call.

**Default fan-out target = 20.** Synthesis emits up to 20 packet seeds split
by work volume. Deterministic synthesizer wraps each with floors/gates/
depends_on from the data dictionary. Fork-out calls run in parallel sharing
a cached prompt prefix (~65% cache hit observed at v23).

**Synthesis is the only allowed sequential step** — it has to write the cache
and produce the seeds before fork-outs can fan. Everything after is parallel.

**v24 direction (Nate, 2026-04-26):**
- Drop `test`/`fix` packets from synthesis output. They're speculative work
  for unapproved plans; authoring them is premature. Fan-out savings ~15-20%
  of cost; cleaner conceptually.
- Use Flash for fork-outs (well-scoped per packet); keep Pro for synthesis
  (decomposition is reasoning work). Halves fork-out cost.
- Replace synthesis "statement" output with a **pill audit** — instead of a
  3-5 sentence narrative, the first model articulates which data pills were
  missed or misattributed. The audit becomes the seed-driver for fork-outs.

**Where this lives in code (as of 2026-04-26):**
`runtime/compose_plan_via_llm.py` orchestrates: `suggest_plan_atoms` →
`synthesize_skeleton` → `synthesize_plan_statement` (1 LLM call → packet_seeds)
→ `skeleton_from_seeds` → `fork_author_packets(concurrency=20)` →
`triage_plan_pills` → `validate_authored_plan`.

The MCP tool `praxis_compose_plan_via_llm` exposes this. The UI "Describe it"
path (compile via `/api/workflows/{id}/build/bootstrap`) currently bypasses
this and does a single `call_llm_compile` instead — that's the bug Nate is
complaining about when he says "how come it loads right away?"
decisions.title: 2026-04-26: When wiring up multi-step compile/plan-authoring, do NOT make the

decisions.rationale: Native host tools and Linux containers do not share the same meaning of localhost. Keep WORKFLOW_DATABASE_URL as the native host authority, allow PRAXIS_DOCKER_WORKFLOW_DATABASE_URL as the Docker-only override for compose services, and only fall back to the native DSN when it is genuinely container-reachable. This prevents split-brain startup failures when Praxis.db is local to the host but the control plane runs in containers.
decisions.title: Compose DB authority is explicit when host and container network paths differ

decisions.rationale: Every system-internal caller that launches or cancels a workflow must go through runtime.control_commands.submit_workflow_command (or ControlCommandType.WORKFLOW_CANCEL via execute_control_intent). Direct calls to runtime.workflow.unified.submit_workflow_inline / cancel_run / cancel_job from triggers, integrations, schedulers, maintenance, dependency chains, or CLI surfaces are forbidden. Reasons: every launch/cancel becomes a durable control_commands row (audit + replay), idempotency is enforced once at the bus, risk classification and operator confirmation are centralized, and operator-visible status converges. The bus has been extended to carry trigger_depth and packet_provenance so loop-protection and integration provenance survive the hop. WORKFLOW_CANCEL accepts either run_id (run-scoped via cancel_run) or job_id (single-job via cancel_job). Sites re-wired in this thread: runtime.integrations.platform.{_submit_workflow_inline,_cancel_workflow_run}, runtime.triggers.{_fire_workflow_trigger,_submit_workflow_for_event}, runtime.database_maintenance, runtime.job_dependencies, surfaces.cli.commands.workflow.queue_cancel.
decisions.title: All in-runtime workflow launches and cancels flow through control_commands

decisions.rationale: Original P0 had 8 objects (added EvidencePacket, AuthorityClaim, ReplayResult, CartridgeRevision). Debate established these are derivable, deferrable, or already implicit in existing receipts/state machinery. Smallest version that proves the precedent loop is 4 objects (Case, Ruling, PrecedentCandidate, PromotedRule). Policy prevents bloat-back of the deferred objects into V0 scope.
decisions.title: P0 object list = {Case, Ruling, PrecedentCandidate, PromotedRule}

decisions.rationale: Praxis data dictionary should be the canonical vocabulary layer for atomic data pills and their meaning, bindings, gates, lineage, trust, and legal use. That vocabulary must connect to bugs, roadmap items, workflows, workflow runs, receipts, prior records, UI surfaces, tools, and external authorities so future LLM runs can resolve the right context and primitives without guessing.
decisions.title: Data dictionary is the shared vocabulary linking pills, context, contracts, bugs, roadmap, runs, and records

decisions.rationale: In Praxis, data pills represent concrete reusable data fields such as first_name, company_domain, api_base_url, or bug_ref. Agent context, provenance, trust level, source authority, legal actions, and gate satisfaction must live in adjacent primitives that describe how a pill may be used. This keeps field vocabulary clean while giving LLMs the context needed to choose correctly.
decisions.title: Data pills are atomic fields; separate context primitives explain meaning, trust, and legal use

decisions.rationale:
Debates should always assign frontier-tier models, one per provider:
- Google: gemini-3.1-pro-preview (NEVER gemini-2.5-pro)
- OpenAI: gpt-5.4 or latest frontier
- Anthropic: claude-opus-4-6 or latest frontier

**Why:** Debates need the strongest reasoning from each provider for real intellectual diversity. Using the same provider multiple times wastes the adversarial structure and risks rate limits. Gemini 2.5 Pro is explicitly banned from debates.

**How to apply:** When writing debate specs, assign each position to a different provider's frontier model. For 6-job debates (3 positions + 3 crossfires), cycle through all three providers twice. If a model isn't in the registry yet, seed model_profiles first.
decisions.title: Debates should always assign frontier-tier models, one per provider:

decisions.rationale:
Operator decisions must carry an explicit, structured scope_clamp ({applies_to: [...], does_not_apply_to: [...]}) as first-class data, not as prose buried in the rationale. Every surface that summarizes, quotes, or references a decision (praxis_orient, compose_plan, launch_plan, agent summaries, plan receipts, closeout receipts, Moon Decisions surface) must reproduce the clamp verbatim — never paraphrase, never summarize, never collapse.

WHY: Decisions get distorted through summarization. Canonical failure: "DeepSeek added for compile-task primary only" got paraphrased to "DeepSeek added to the pool," and the broader (false) claim later spread to other call sites and infrastructure decisions. Free-text scope buried in rationale is invisible to downstream LLMs and agents — they paraphrase the body and the clamp evaporates. Structured scope as queryable data is the only way the clamp survives the chain of summaries.

BINDS:
- operator_decisions schema gains a required scope_clamp JSONB column ({applies_to: [string], does_not_apply_to: [string]}). Existing rows backfill from rationale or get an explicit pending_review placeholder. Migration filed under workflow/ as a follow-up to this decision.
- All surfaces that emit decision references must include scope_clamp verbatim. Plans, summaries, and orient envelopes that reference a decision without quoting its clamp are invalid; the validator rejects them and the receipt fails.
- Moon exposes a Decisions surface — recency-sorted, all decision_kind values, clamp visible — so the operator can see what is binding without leaving the canvas. CLI and SQL access remain, but Moon is the primary operator-facing surface.
- Lifting/superseding a decision uses the existing named-only rule (memory feedback_supersede_only_named); structured scope clamps prevent accidental over-broad lifts during supersession.

SCOPE_CLAMP (eating own dog food, encoded inline until the column exists):
- applies_to:
  - All operator_decisions rows across every decision_kind (architecture_policy, delivery_plan, dataset_promotion, etc.)
  - All surfaces that reference, summarize, or quote decisions: praxis_orient, compose_plan, launch_plan, plan_lifecycle, agent summaries, plan receipts, closeout receipts, Moon Decisions surface, status snapshots
  - Agents and LLMs producing plans, summaries, recommendations, or roadmap proposals that lean on decision authority
- does_not_apply_to:
  - Conversation context that has not yet been promoted to a decision row
  - User feedback in chat that has not gone through praxis_operator_write or praxis_operator_decisions(action=record)
  - Auto-memory entries — those have their own scope discipline (the user's MEMORY.md index)
  - Raw query receipts (run_status, query results) that surface a decision_ref pointer without summarizing it

COMPATIBLE WITH (does not supersede):
- decision-hygiene::file-decisions-inline (filing must now include scope_clamp)
- decision-tables::operator-decisions-authority (scope_clamp lives on that table)
- decision-tables::queryable-control (clamp is queryable JSONB)

applies_to: All operator_decisions rows across every decision_kind (architecture_policy, delivery_plan, dataset_promotion, etc.)

All surfaces that reference, summarize, or quote decisions: praxis_orient, compose_plan, launch_plan, plan_lifecycle, agent summaries, plan receipts, closeout receipts, Moon Decisions surface, status snapshots
Agents and LLMs producing plans, summaries, recommendations, or roadmap proposals that lean on decision authority
does_not_apply_to: Conversation context that has not yet been promoted to a decision row
User feedback in chat that has not gone through praxis_operator_write or praxis_operator_decisions(action=record)
Auto-memory entries — those have their own scope discipline (the user's MEMORY.md index)
Raw query receipts (run_status, query results) that surface a decision_ref pointer without summarizing it

decisions.title: Decisions carry structured scope_clamp; surfaces quote it verbatim, never paraphrase

decisions.rationale: Architecture decisions made in conversation must become DB-backed operator records immediately. Chat-only or doc-only decisions are not durable authority for future runs.
decisions.title: File durable decisions through operator authority as they are made

decisions.rationale: Every dataset_promotion, dataset_rejection, and dataset_promotion_supersede must write a typed operator_decisions row with dataset-scoped scope_ref. Free-text rationale and legacy decision_ref columns on dataset_promotions are supplemental; the decision row is the authority.
decisions.title: Dataset refinery promotions are typed operator decisions

decisions.rationale: Do not script around architecture that should live in the database. Authority, durable state, orchestration, event relationships, registry metadata, and runtime coordination must live in durable DB primitives rather than ad hoc scripts.
decisions.title: Decision tables are DB-native authority

decisions.rationale: Cross-cutting architecture policy guidance belongs in operator_decisions under the typed architecture_policy kind. If the decision table shape needs cleanup, improve operator_decisions rather than creating a parallel decision store.
decisions.title: Operator decisions is the authority table

decisions.rationale: The runtime, event bus, and registry should be backed by durable, inspectable, queryable DB primitives where appropriate so control over state is real and observable.
decisions.title: Decision-table control must stay inspectable and queryable

decisions.rationale: Use scripts for tooling and support tasks such as migrations, smoke checks, and maintenance helpers. Do not implement core system behavior in ad hoc scripts just because it is faster in the moment.
decisions.title: Scripts support decision tables; they do not replace them

decisions.rationale: If the decision-table shape is too complex, simplify or improve operator_decisions itself. Do not introduce a parallel decision store for architecture policy or operator control guidance.
decisions.title: Use operator_decisions as the decision table authority

decisions.rationale: `docker restart` doesn't re-resolve `${VAR:-}` from compose. To pick up new env: `eval "$(bin/praxis-compose-env)"` then `docker-compose up -d --force-recreate <svc>`. Repeated restart cycles leave workers with stale empty token.
decisions.title: docker restart preserves cached env; use compose recreate

decisions.rationale:
Decision made 2026-04-19: rather than buy a Mac mini during the DRAM price spike, move Praxis Engine to a rented VPS. Local Mac becomes thin client (editor + SSH + MCP bridge).

**Why:** RAM/DRAM prices are irrational in 2026 Q1-Q2; Praxis standing orders already declare execution as "containers-as-muscle, authority stays in Praxis.db" — remote execution is aligned with the architecture, not a workaround.

**Ship gate:** by 2026-07-19, one external human (not Nate) must be using some Praxis surface. If not, Praxis is reclassified as hobby and spending stops. If yes, DRAM should have normalized enough to buy the Mini in Q4.

**How to apply:**
- When Nate asks infrastructure questions, default to VPS-hosted answers, not "buy bigger local hardware."
- When scoping new Praxis work, ask: does this move the ship-gate needle? If not, flag it.
- Backup + recovery is load-bearing — he has no local copy if VPS dies, so dumps to external object storage are non-negotiable.
decisions.title: Decision made 2026-04-19: rather than buy a Mac mini during the DRAM price spike, move Praxis Engine to a rented VPS. Lo

decisions.rationale:
The `praxis-worker:latest` Docker image (`Code&DBs/Workflow/docker/praxis-worker.Dockerfile`) ships node + claude/codex/gemini/cursor CLIs + python3 — but NO `praxis` binary. Any workflow-spec prompt that tells the agent to `subprocess.run(["praxis", "workflow", "tools", "call", ...])` will fail with "command not found" inside the container.

**Correct pattern** (demonstrated by `artifacts/_archive_v5_hubspot_enrichment_20260418/_build_snapshot.py`):

```python
import json, os, sys, urllib.parse, urllib.request
sys.path.insert(0, '/workspace/Code&DBs/Workflow')
from runtime.workflow.mcp_session import mint_workflow_mcp_session_token
bundle = json.loads(os.environ['PRAXIS_EXECUTION_BUNDLE'])
allowed = bundle.get('mcp_tool_names', [])
token = mint_workflow_mcp_session_token(
    run_id=bundle.get('run_id'), workflow_id=bundle.get('workflow_id'),
    job_label=bundle.get('job_label',''), allowed_tools=allowed,
)
MCP_URL = f"http://host.docker.internal:8420/mcp?allowed_tools={urllib.parse.quote(','.join(allowed))}&workflow_token={urllib.parse.quote(token)}"
# POST JSON-RPC tools/call payloads to MCP_URL
```

**Why:** The worker is isolated from host Keychain, Postgres, and the `praxis` script. The MCP bridge is the ONE authorized channel for reaching praxis_* tools from inside a worker. The bridge validates the session token against `allowed_tools` admitted in the execution bundle.

**How to apply:** When writing or reviewing a spec whose jobs touch integrations (HubSpot, Stripe, etc.), ensure the prompt uses the MCP bridge wrapper. Reject any prompt that invokes `praxis workflow tools call` via subprocess from inside a `task_type=build|research|analysis|architecture` job. The `general` / `integration` task types that execute host-side are the only contexts where the `praxis` CLI is reachable.

**Regression trace:** `W_hubspot_deal_enrichment_20260418.queue.json` v6 regressed this pattern and produced repeated `sandbox_error` receipts (BUG-52C7C2F7, BUG-923C0EA6). v7 restored the MCP-bridge pattern.
decisions.title: The `praxis-worker:latest` Docker image (`Code&DBs/Workflow/docker/praxis-worker.Dockerfile`) ships node + claude/codex/

decisions.rationale: Do not replace library or runtime choice by rebuilding tokenizer, model, pooling, or normalization behavior ourselves. Backend changes must happen behind the embedding contract instead of creating bespoke inference maintenance debt.
decisions.title: Do not hand-roll embedding inference

decisions.rationale: Do not remove semantic compile, query, or discover behavior without a validated replacement. Torch remains the compatibility backend until a leaner backend such as ONNX proves the same embedding contract and acceptable retrieval quality.
decisions.title: Semantic capability must survive backend swaps

decisions.rationale: Treat local embedding inference as a build/runtime boundary problem, not an API-shape problem. Keep semantic capability at the API and worker contracts, but host heavy local inference in a dedicated semantic backend instead of bundling torch into default control-plane images.
decisions.title: Semantic inference leaves default control-plane images

decisions.rationale: Conceptual events registered in operation_catalog_registry (event_required=TRUE, event_type set) emit through operation_catalog_gateway to authority_events with receipt_id linkage. system_events remains a sidecar observability stream during the transition window for plan.launched / plan.composed (and any other events whose handlers still call emit_system_event). When both streams carry the same event, authority_events is the canonical source — receipt linkage, replay policy, and CQRS contract live there. Sidecar removal happens after consumers (Moon observability, replay tooling) migrate their reads. Until then, dedupe on operation_ref + emitted_at when joining both streams.
decisions.title: authority_events is the canonical source for receipt-backed conceptual events

decisions.rationale: Moon, observability, and future agents should not infer programs only from N workflow_run rows. The runtime must emit conceptual events for meaningful trust-compiler transitions: plan.launched, composer.compiled, graph.mutated, wave.launched, typed_gap.created, projection.reconciled, trust_drift.detected. These events are durable authority for supervision, replay, UI updates, roadmap/bug linkage, and LLM context packets.
decisions.title: Conceptual events are first-class operator events above raw run rows

decisions.rationale: Phone and remote agent control must persist session identity, CLI resume id, transcript events, lifecycle status, revocation, and activity receipts in Praxis.db. Filesystem JSONL under artifacts/agents may remain a compatibility export for local inspection, but it is not the source of truth for persistent CLI conversations.
decisions.title: Interactive CLI sessions are DB-backed authority, not filesystem authority

decisions.rationale: The mobile agent path must not default to free OpenRouter models for operator conversations. Live probing on 2026-04-24 showed qwen/qwen3-coder supports tool calls, answered quickly, and is inexpensive enough for mobile operator chat while avoiding the free-tier route. The active default is provider=openrouter, model=qwen/qwen3-coder; provider/model must remain visible in the mobile UI and response events.
decisions.title: Mobile agent sessions default to a paid open tool-capable model

decisions.rationale: Mobile conversations must not depend on a single desktop CLI credential state. The DB-backed agent session remains the authority, while each session records an explicit provider and can route through a working CLI transport such as Codex or an API transport such as OpenRouter. Provider failures must return bounded, inspectable events instead of silent hangs.
decisions.title: Mobile agent sessions are provider-aware and may use CLI or API transports

decisions.rationale: Phone access must be modeled as an installable operator surface with DB-backed session authority and pairing. Tailscale, LAN, or internet exposure are transport choices only; they must not become the product authority or the session model.
decisions.title: Mobile operator access is an app surface, not a tunnel contract

decisions.rationale: Mobile approval must approve the existing DB-backed workflow.submit control command and then execute that command. It must not create a second launch path, skip command status transitions, or infer approval from chat text. This preserves requested/accepted/running/succeeded receipts and lets status URLs, stream URLs, and telemetry remain the authority.
decisions.title: Mobile approval releases workflow control commands

decisions.rationale: The phone app may request workflow launches, but it must not become a hidden shell or parallel runner. Mobile launch requests compile into canonical prompt workflow specs and submit through the DB-backed workflow command bus so approvals, idempotency, receipts, status URLs, and run telemetry stay authoritative and queryable.
decisions.title: Mobile workflow launches use the workflow command bus

decisions.rationale: API admission for the private control plane previously lived in two parallel authorities: task_type_routing rank ordering and private_provider_api_job_allowlist on/off gating. Keeping them in sync was the failure mode that produced the migration 282 column-rename bootstrap jam, the routing-vs-allowlist drift the task_type_routing_admission_audit view was created to surface, and the BUG-2062CB3B / BUG-110F4EA3 / BUG-ECD0E5B3 family of three-table CHECK-trigger foot-guns. As of migrations 286 + 287 (2026-04-27), task_type_routing carries transport_type (CLI|API) and sub_task_type ('*' default; concrete values reserved for compile sub-stages) directly. The private_model_access_control_matrix view derives API admission from a routing JOIN filtered to transport_type='API' instead of the legacy allowlist join. Adding a new (task_type, provider, model) on a transport now means writing one routing row, not two rows that have to agree across tables. The legacy private_provider_api_job_allowlist table remains in place but has no consumers and is queued for retire in a follow-up roadmap item.
decisions.title: task_type_routing is the only API admission authority

decisions.rationale:
Moon is the entire Praxis UI. Not a tab, not a builder surface — the app.

**Core principles:**
- `/app` → Moon. No separate dashboard, no separate RunDetailView, no separate pages.
- Every interaction writes to Postgres via API. Nothing is local-only state that evaporates on refresh.
- The catalog is real — items map to actual engine capabilities (fan-out, conditional branching, retry, sub-workflow invoke, integration bindings).
- Runs render in Moon — same canvas, same nodes, same renderer. Click a node to see its output.
- Build → dispatch → watch results. One flow, one place. Same canvas switches to showing live execution.
- One server (`localhost:8420/app`), self-healing, no port confusion.
- No shims, no legacy bridges, no local-only state, no catch-all routes, no adapter patterns.

**Why:** The user's vision is "circles, lines, from nothing and beginning to be filled — deep description in simplistically simple at first glance diagrams." Direction of flow means something. Gates drag in external capabilities. Moon is the authoritative surface where workflows are built, dispatched, and monitored.

**How to apply:** Never build a separate page for anything Moon should show. Never add local-only state — persist to DB. Never add adapter/shim layers. When something doesn't work, fix the root cause in Moon, not by routing to a different view.
decisions.title: Moon is the entire Praxis UI. Not a tab, not a builder surface — the app.

decisions.rationale:
Moon UI visual direction validated by user (2026-04-18):

- **Mostly black + white.** No solid green/accent rings at rest. State color only when state is active (running/succeeded/failed in an actual run).
- **Hollow circles.** Nodes keep their dark interior — the icon sits inside, the ring is thin and white. Keep lots of visible "black space."
- **Subtle glow, not fill.** Light-source glow in a muted hue (blue, amber, etc.) can encode node *type*, but only at ~10-15% intensity — a whisper, not a shout.
- **Compact-idle, expand-on-interaction.** Validated specifically for gate pods (28px dashed circle at rest, expands to full pill on hover/selected/drag-over). Pattern is reusable for other dense canvas primitives.
- **Shape vocabulary is on the table** for distinguishing node roles (step vs trigger vs gate) but must stay consistent with the monochrome aesthetic.

**Why:** User wants the canvas to feel spacious and quiet at rest, with meaning emerging through interaction and live state, not through persistent decoration.

**How to apply:** When designing Moon primitives, default to monochrome + hollow + compact. Reserve color and size for live state or active interaction. Don't paint identity on every element.
decisions.title: Moon UI visual direction validated by user (2026-04-18):

decisions.rationale: Five enabled plan-composition operation rows pointed to runtime modules that no longer exist in the source tree, leaving only stale __pycache__ artifacts. Enabled operation-catalog rows must resolve to live source-backed Pydantic models and handlers before they can mount as API capabilities. Rows without source-backed bindings are disabled until rebuilt through a durable migration or runtime owner.
decisions.title: Disable operation-catalog rows whose Python bindings are not backed by source

decisions.rationale: POST /api/surface/action was registered as surface.action.performed in operation_catalog_registry but also had a static API wrapper route. That created duplicated HTTP authority and blocked capability mounting. The operation-catalog route is the canonical HTTP owner; execution still flows through operation_catalog_gateway and emits the receipt/event.
decisions.title: Surface action HTTP route is owned by operation catalog

decisions.rationale: Operator explicitly directed that the phone console remove model choices and use DeepSeek V4 Pro. This is a narrow operator-console chat exception to the private API compile-only default; Tailscale remains the access boundary and no general provider/model picker is exposed.
decisions.title: Operator console chat uses Together DeepSeek V4 Pro as the single lane

decisions.rationale: Every operator_decisions row exposes (1) decision_provenance ∈ {explicit, inferred} and (2) optional decision_why separate from rationale. Explicit = operator unequivocally said so. Inferred = model guessed during conversation/debate parsing. Surfacing layers (trigger registry render, friction events, compliance scoring, conflict resolution) weight explicit higher than inferred. Drillable means surfaces include enough metadata (or a fetch hint) for consumers to pull the full object — title, rationale, why, scope_clamp, history.
decisions.title: Decisions are drillable; provenance and why are first-class

decisions.rationale: When Praxis is invited into repo-local or sensitive operational systems, first-run setup must capture operator SOPs, forbidden actions, sensitive-system handling rules, and initial anti-pattern examples as durable contract authority rather than leaving them in chat residue. When future behavior is logged as a pattern or a bug is promoted/resolved into anti-pattern authority, the product must say so plainly during the early interactions so operators understand what memory and guardrail value they are building.
decisions.title: First-run onboarding must capture repo policy contracts and disclose pattern promotion behavior

decisions.rationale: Fresh operator sessions must begin from the runtime authority envelope instead of sidecar docs. The envelope binds standing orders, workspace boundary, database authority, health, and live operator surfaces so future runs have one queryable source of truth.
decisions.title: Orient is the mandatory runtime authority envelope

decisions.rationale: Recurring platform pain should materialize as durable, queryable pattern authority that clusters receipts, friction events, typed gaps, and bugs before creating or duplicating bug work. Bugs remain concrete fix records; patterns own recurring system shapes, evidence links, promotion rules, and intervention state so the platform becomes less reactive than bug tickets alone.
decisions.title: Failure patterns are durable authority between evidence and bugs

decisions.rationale: Every LLM run should receive a canonical agent operating packet before acting. The packet is the trust compiler output: current goal, source_refs, relevant data pills and values, pill context, source anchors, UI surfaces, known broken outcomes, related bugs and roadmap, legal next actions, blocked actions, required gates, trust levels, receipts, external edges, write_envelope, expected verification, and human decisions needed. Chat, Moon, workflow specs, and CLI surfaces are views over or consumers of this packet, not replacements for it.
decisions.title: Agent operating packet is the primary LLM interface

decisions.rationale: Corrects a CQRS bypass in Phase 1.1.d. plan.launched was implemented via emit_system_event → direct INSERT INTO system_events, which is the observability sidecar half of the event story — NOT the CQRS-proper registration. Per migration 204 (cqrs_event_projection_contracts), authority_event_contracts rows are auto-seeded from operation_catalog_registry rows whose event_type is declared. So every conceptual event (plan.launched, composer.compiled, graph.mutated, wave.launched, typed_gap.created, projection.reconciled, trust_drift.detected) must: (1) have an operation_catalog_registry entry declaring event_type; (2) emission flows through operation_receipt.event_ids (the canonical receipt-backed event path); (3) system_events remains the freeform observability sidecar, NOT the authoritative event store. Generalization: every write surface must route through operation_catalog_registry (command side) + CQRS event contracts (observation side). Parallel emission paths (direct INSERTs to system_events, ad-hoc service_bus writes, uncatalogued writes to projection tables) bypass authority and create drift between what agents can see and what actually happened. Pairs with data-dictionary-universal-compile-time-clamp policy: the dictionary types the state, operation_catalog types the operations that mutate state, authority_event_contracts types the events that observe state. All three clamps required. Queued follow-up: register launch_plan (or a plan-aggregate operation) in operation_catalog_registry; let migration 204 seed auto-create the authority_event_contract row; route emission through the operation gateway.
decisions.title: Conceptual events must register through operation_catalog_registry, not parallel emission paths

decisions.rationale:
Every Praxis Engine operation MUST run through operation_catalog_gateway.execute_operation_from_subsystems, with its definition (handler_ref, input_model_ref, output_schema_ref, authority_domain_ref, idempotency_policy, receipt_required, event_required, event_type) registered as a row in operation_catalog_registry — not as ad-hoc MCP-tool dispatches or direct subsystem calls.

Why: a future with hundreds of concurrent agents demands robust determinism — same input → same outcome → same durable proof — with no shim layers between intent and authority. The gateway pattern delivers this through:

  1. Pydantic input_model_ref validation at the seam, so malformed payloads fail before touching state.
  2. authority_operation_receipts records every call: input hash, idempotency key, started/finished timestamps, execution_status (completed | replayed | failed). This is the audit ledger and the replay substrate.
  3. idempotency_policy ('non_idempotent' | 'idempotent' | 'read_only') is explicit per operation, so duplicate dispatches replay cached results when safe and re-execute when not.
  4. authority_events emission for command operations with event_required=TRUE creates a typed conceptual-event stream that lenses (Moon, Atlas, observability) subscribe to instead of polling — pairs with architecture_policy::atlas_moon_live_updates::event_stream_driven_graph_lenses.
  5. authority_object_registry + data_dictionary_objects rows make every operation queryable, lineage-traversable, and visible to typed-edge walks — pairs with architecture_policy::outcome_graph::typed_edges.

Implications, binding on every future build packet:
- No MCP-tier shims dispatching to subsystems directly. MCP tools are thin wrappers that call execute_operation_from_subsystems and return the gateway's result. Caught two such shims this session (praxis_search and praxis_run_*) and migrated them to gateway-dispatched.
- Migrations registering new operations must populate all three CQRS tables consistently: operation_catalog_registry (operation_kind, idempotency_policy, receipt_required, event_required), authority_object_registry (object_kind matching operation_kind), data_dictionary_objects (category matching operation_kind). The 'always command' workaround captured in BUG-36B00C79 is retired — use 'query' for query operations, 'command' for command operations.
- Read receipts are not optional even for queries — the same per-call ledger that audits writes must audit reads, so an agent's "what did the LLM ask?" trail is reproducible.

Pairs with: architecture_policy::event_architecture::authority_events_canonical_source_for_receipt_backed_conceptual_events; architecture_policy::outcome_graph::typed_edges; architecture_policy::atlas_moon_live_updates::event_stream_driven_graph_lenses.

Origin: operator confirmation 2026-04-26 during the search consolidation lift (migration 278 + 279). Search.federated and 9 per-source search query operations are the first batch registered under this policy; the same shape now binds every future Engine operation.
decisions.title: CQRS gateway dispatch is the engine bus — robust determinism for the hundreds-of-agents future

decisions.rationale: The data dictionary already has real bones: layered authority (auto / inferred / operator, operator wins), effective views, field definitions, lineage edges (references, derives_from, projects_to, ingests_from, produces, consumes, promotes_to, same_as), quality rules and quality runs, classifications, stewardship, governance scans (can file bugs), wiring audit, CQRS query surfaces, MCP/HTTP tools, deterministic intent binding for explicit object.field refs. Today it is mostly a vocabulary/governance/projection authority. The next move is to make it a REQUIRED compile-time clamp across: workflow compilation, tool selection, Moon graph generation, plan packet typing, MCP/CLI/API primitive discovery, verifier selection, UI geometry, source-code anchors, roadmap linkage, run-live status, actual write-set reconciliation, legal next action computation. Do not build parallel abstractions (type_contract dicts in tool files, ad-hoc registries, separate typing stacks) — wire the dictionary harder. It is the semantic join layer for data pills, pill context, tool contracts, gates, bugs, roadmap items, workflow definitions, workflow runs, receipts, previous records, UI surfaces, source anchors, and external authorities.
decisions.title: Data dictionary is the universal compile-time clamp; wire it harder, don't build around it

decisions.rationale: Terminology correction. Data pills are ATOMIC DATA FIELDS: first_name, company_domain, api_base_url, auth_type, bug_ref, repo_path, selector, viewport_width, credential_ref, etc. Not context bundles. The context around pills is modeled as separate first-class primitives: DataPill (field definition), DataPillValue (concrete value in a specific subject/run/workflow), PillContext (meaning, usage, warnings, legal/unsafe uses), PillBinding (where the pill maps into tools, workflows, UI, APIs, DB columns, MCP/CLI/API inputs), PillGate (proof required before the pill can be trusted for certain actions), PillLineage (where the value came from and what changed it), PillConflict (competing values for the same field), PillDefault (safe defaults by pack/domain), PillPromptHint (how to ask/present the field), PillRedactionPolicy (whether it can enter prompts/logs/UI/receipts). The data dictionary is the canonical vocabulary join layer tying pills to tool contracts, gates, bugs, roadmap items, workflow definitions, runs, receipts, previous records, UI surfaces, source anchors, external authorities. Prevents drift where "pill" gets conflated with richer structures and the dictionary's semantic join authority gets bypassed.
decisions.title: DataPill primitives — atomic fields plus separate context primitives

decisions.rationale: Refines architecture-policy::platform-architecture::data-pill-primitive-family. The original policy enumerated 10 DataPill primitives (DataPill, DataPillValue, PillContext, PillBinding, PillGate, PillLineage, PillConflict, PillDefault, PillPromptHint, PillRedactionPolicy). Auditing against existing Praxis substrate shows most of these already have authority-backed homes — creating parallel tables would violate architecture-policy::platform-architecture::data-dictionary-universal-compile-time-clamp ("wire the dictionary harder, don't build parallel abstractions"). Correct mapping: (1) DataPill = data_dictionary_entries rows keyed on (object_kind, field_path). field_kind already covers type; label/description cover meaning. (2) DataPillValue = values observed in workflow_run / receipt payloads; no separate table needed — the value is where it flows, not a separately-persisted mirror. (3) PillContext = data_dictionary_entries.description + metadata JSON. (4) PillBinding = data_dictionary_lineage (migration 169) for field-to-field wiring + operation_catalog_registry input_schema_ref / output_schema_ref for tool-to-field wiring. (5) PillGate = existing gate_evaluations + future gate_instances rows scoped to pill fields. (6) PillLineage = data_dictionary_lineage authoritatively. (7) PillDefault = data_dictionary_entries.default_value. (8) PillPromptHint = data_dictionary_entries.examples (list of canonical prompts). The primitives that genuinely need new authority: (a) PillConflict — represents "competing values for the same field across sources". Candidate table: data_dictionary_conflicts (not yet designed). (b) PillRedactionPolicy — whether the value may appear in prompts/logs/UI/receipts. Candidate: a column on data_dictionary_classifications OR a dedicated pill_redaction_policies table. Phase 1.4 is therefore NOT "build 10 tables" but "map 8 primitives to existing authority, design 2 new ones". Concrete follow-ups: wire praxis_data_dictionary entries as the canonical DataPill read surface; add data_dictionary_conflicts + pill_redaction_policies tables only when a concrete consumer (Moon, compose_plan, LLM binding) forces them. Keeps the DataPill vocabulary real without proliferating tables that duplicate existing authority.
decisions.title: DataPill primitive family maps to existing authority, not 10 new tables

decisions.rationale: Refines prior policy "post-hoc-derivation-over-pre-declared-guess." Caller lightness (description-only intent at the caller boundary) does NOT mean compiler lightness. The trust compiler must still derive a pre-run ENVELOPE before launch. Three surfaces: (1) pre-run envelope = compiled expectation — write_envelope (allowed/expected scope), expected_verification (compiler expectation), expected_gates, expected_capabilities; (2) post-run truth = runtime-observed — write_set (actual touched files/objects), actual_verification (derived from actual write_set), satisfied_gates (by evidence); (3) delta = finding — mismatch between expected and actual becomes a typed gap or receipt, never silent drift. Example: PlanPacket caller provides description only; compiler derives write_envelope from source_refs + catalog; runtime observes write_set; divergence emits typed_gap or trust_drift event. This separation is what lets caller input stay minimal while compiler authority stays strict. Compile = declare expected + observe actual + reconcile.
decisions.title: Pre-run envelope (expected) is distinct from post-run truth (actual); mismatch is a finding

decisions.rationale: Silent defaults are the worst of both worlds: they look like success while shipping untested or unrouted work. Examples to eliminate: _generate_verify_refs silently returning [] for non-Python files (ships JS/TS/SQL/MD untested); launch_plan's auto/<stage> fallback when no admitted route exists (ships unrouted agents); Moon composer's auto/build catch-all on prose nodes (ships unrunnable graph). The contract is: if a typed concrete value cannot be produced at compile, fail with a clear error OR produce a typed gap primitive with legal repair actions. Never silent-zero. Never a generic fallback that masquerades as work. This is the trust boundary between compiler and pretty checklist — the whole LLM-first operator model depends on refusing convenience that lies.
decisions.title: Fail closed at compile, never silent-zero at runtime

decisions.rationale: The trust compiler must turn current typed state into a finite set of legal next actions. LLMs should not receive the whole tool universe when state can narrow choices. Legal actions are computed from data dictionary fields, tool contracts, consumes/produces types, gates, source_refs, trust levels, write_envelope, and external-edge status. If no legal action exists, emit a typed gap with repair actions. This is the mechanism that makes the right choice easiest and prevents open-world tool search.
decisions.title: Legal action graph is required compiler output, not optional UX

decisions.rationale: Anchors the "legal" term used across the trust compiler substrate. Legal is NOT an ethics/ToS/permission concept. Legal = "the preconditions are satisfied by the current typed state, so this action can fire without producing a typed gap." The trust compiler computes legality from these ingredients, in priority order: (1) consumes/produces types — does what the action needs match what the accumulator already has (per source-refs-plural-canonical-shape + data-dictionary-universal-compile-time-clamp); (2) data dictionary fields — are the required data pills present with the right shape (DataPill ≡ data_dictionary_entries per data-pill-primitives-map-to-existing-authority); (3) gates — have all required approvals / validations been satisfied (gate_evaluations authority); (4) source_refs — does the authority backing exist (bugs / roadmap / idea / friction / decision / review / discovery); (5) trust levels — future Phase 3 (column not yet on operation_catalog); (6) write_envelope — would the write stay within declared invariants (per expected-envelope-vs-actual-truth-separation); (7) external-edge status — is the external integration reachable and healthy. If ANY fail, the action is illegal in this state and the compiler emits a typed_gap.created event (per conceptual-events-register-through-operation-catalog-registry + fail-closed-at-compile-no-silent-defaults). Applications: next_legal_tools(state) on the command side (narrow from 100-tool search to graph walk); legal_modules_for(workspace_context) on the query side (narrow the render palette to modules whose consumes types match currently bound pills); legal surface_bundle for workflow_run (a bundle is legal only if every quadrant's projection resolves against the run's current state). Legal ≠ permitted: permitted is "is the operator / agent authorized to do this" (trust budget, operator_decisions, authz). Legal is "can this compile to a non-gap output." Both matter; they are separable concerns.
decisions.title: Legal = computable to a non-gap output (not permitted)

decisions.rationale: Clarifies architecture-policy::platform-architecture::legal-action-graph-is-required-compiler-output. Legal in Praxis is a term of art, not ethics/ToS. An action is legal iff its preconditions are satisfied by current typed state such that execution would produce a non-gap output. Ingredients (per the legal-action-graph policy): consumes/produces types, data dictionary fields, gates, source_refs, write_envelope, external-edge status. Trust level is an ingredient only once the column exists (Phase 3). If any ingredient fails, the action is illegal in this state, and the compiler MUST emit a typed_gap row with repair actions (never hide the button silently; fail-closed). Naming symmetry: command-side canonical term is 'legal next actions' (from architecture-policy::platform-architecture::agent-operating-packet-is-primary-llm-interface, which has the agent operating packet surface legal next actions, blocked actions, and required gates). Query-side term mirrors this as legal_modules(state), legal_sources(state), legal_bundle_for(workflow_run). The adjective is legal; no next_ prefix, because nextness is implicit in state evaluation. Consequences for builders: (1) any rendering of 'available X' (modules, tools, actions, source options) for an LLM or for Moon must compute legality from typed state, not show the open-world catalog; (2) illegality must land as a typed_gap row queryable by 'what is illegal here and why', not as disabled UI state; (3) repair actions returned alongside the typed_gap are the compiler way of making an illegal action legal. They are themselves legal actions that, if taken, change state so the originally-illegal action becomes legal.
decisions.title: Legal = computable to non-gap output given current typed state; not permission; query-side term is legal_modules(state) matching command-side legal next actions

decisions.rationale: For Praxis operator and LLM-facing architecture, the bound moment-in-time truth object handed to or consumed by a run is called a manifest. It is explicit, inspectable, and operationally meaningful for that moment. Legacy packet terminology may remain in implementation file names, stored revisions, or migration-era helper APIs until separately migrated, but operator-facing language should prefer manifest to avoid UI metaphor drift and to better express assembled authoritative truth.
decisions.title: Manifest is the canonical term for the ephemeral moment-of-truth execution bundle

decisions.rationale: Praxis is an agent substrate, not a workflow builder. One graph lives in Praxis.db (nodes, edges, gates, typed by data-dictionary consumes/produces). Every surface is a lens on that same graph: Moon canvas = live render of graph rows; executor = interpreter walking the rows; CLI/MCP/HTTP = alternate lenses on the same rows; NL authoring = grammar that mutates rows; "spec" = export format, not source of truth; "run" = graph with status + cursor overlay. Composition is prescriptive — at any state, the graph narrows to the 3-5 tools whose consumes type matches the accumulator. LLM planning collapses from 100-tool search to a graph walk. Moon steering shows only legal next steps. The user (Nate) does not code. Agents build and mutate the graph; the user steers — approves gates, edits a misbehaving node — does not assemble nodes from a palette. Moon is the control room, not an IDE. When "edit in Moon" and "next run's behavior" are the same DB write, the shim between UI and runtime is dead. Any feature that reintroduces a translation layer between a surface and the graph is a regression. Positioning implication: do not compete with Zapier on connector count — position as agent control plane for one person, where agents handle breadth and the user steers via Moon.
decisions.title: One graph, many lenses

decisions.rationale: When multiple source authorities can seed the same downstream shape (PlanPacket, graph subtree, operating packet, composer intent), use ONE polymorphic `from:` field that dispatches by ref prefix, not N parallel shortcut methods (from_bugs, from_roadmap, from_idea, from_friction, from_review, from_decision, from_discovery). Each authority resolves its own defaults through its own surface — a bug knows its registry_paths and write_set_diff; a roadmap_item knows its acceptance criteria; an idea knows its scope hints. The caller provides intent; the system dispatches to the right authority and hydrates.
