This keeps the primary shape minimal, makes new source authorities additive (not a new method), and enforces the principle that authorities own their own metadata. Applies to: launch_plan.from, compose_plan_from_intent.from, agent_context_packet.from_ref, Moon composer intent input.
decisions.title: One polymorphic `from:` field, not N shortcut methods

decisions.rationale: Pre-declared fields rot. Post-hoc derived fields are grounded in what actually happened. When a value can be observed at runtime (verify_refs from actual write_set, capabilities from what actually ran, gate satisfaction from actual evidence, type flow from observed consumes/produces), derive it post-hoc rather than making the caller pre-declare. Declared values are acceptable as hints, never as requirements. Concrete implications: _generate_verify_refs should derive from write_set at completion, not from intended write at compile; Moon node contracts should pair declared type_contract with observed run behavior; gate satisfaction is proven by evidence, not claimed by intent. Pairs with source-linked-outcome-graph: the repo is the script, observed behavior is the outcome. Caller tax drops; truth quality rises.
decisions.title: Derive at completion, not declare at compile

decisions.rationale: Correction and refinement of prior policy "polymorphic-from-over-n-shortcuts." The polymorphism principle stays right (one caller-facing field dispatches by ref prefix). The cardinality was wrong. Canonical internal shape must support MULTIPLE parents: source_refs: ["BUG-X", "roadmap_item.Y", "decision.Z", "review.V"]. A plan packet, graph subtree, operating packet, or composer intent can legitimately inherit from several source authorities simultaneously (a bug filed against a roadmap item backed by a decision reviewed in V). Caller convenience input may accept singular from: "BUG-X" — dispatched internally to source_refs = ["BUG-X"]. Each source authority still resolves its own defaults through its own surface. New source authorities remain additive, not a new method. Supersedes the singular cardinality in the prior polymorphic-from policy; spirit preserved, shape plural.
decisions.title: source_refs: [] is the canonical internal shape; from: is caller convenience

decisions.rationale: A task_environment_contract should not be generated from scratch every time or cached until silently stale. It is a versioned manifest of what success means for a task/business process, assembled from human knowledge, SOPs, anti-patterns, current data, historical evidence, trust constraints, and model/tool context. Each use should reference the prior contract, bind fresh evidence, compute a new hash, append a revision when material facts or rules change, and preserve failure anti-patterns and verifier outcomes as contract evolution evidence. This gives Claude Code-level on-the-fly capability a trustworthy business substrate: flexible execution over explicit, inspectable, replayable success contracts.
decisions.title: Task environment contracts are reused and evolved as append-only hashed manifests

decisions.rationale: Events are the command-event ledger emitted by every gateway command with event_required=TRUE. Deleting them breaks downstream projections and event-sourced consumers. Enforced via BEFORE DELETE + BEFORE TRUNCATE triggers.
decisions.title: authority_events is immutable

decisions.rationale: Standing orders are the durable history of operator authority. To retire a decision, set effective_to (supersession). Hard delete erases the audit trail and lets a confused agent rewrite history. Enforced at the data layer via a BEFORE DELETE trigger.
decisions.title: operator_decisions rows are not deletable

decisions.rationale: Receipts are the canonical audit trail for every gateway dispatch. Deleting them breaks replay, cause-walking, and idempotency caches. Enforced via BEFORE DELETE + BEFORE TRUNCATE triggers.
decisions.title: authority_operation_receipts is immutable

decisions.rationale: Authorities, engines, gateway wrappers, and repositories follow the same shape across the platform.  Declaring each one as a primitive_catalog row makes the inventory queryable, lets a consistency scanner detect blueprint-vs-code drift (orphaned authorities, ghost catalog rows, missing tests), and creates the seam for a future scaffolder that mints a new primitive from one row.  Recursive: the primitive_catalog primitive is itself in the catalog, demonstrating the contract.
decisions.title: Platform primitives are declared in primitive_catalog and consistency-checked against code

decisions.rationale: Core product thesis: Praxis should not require a solo builder to manually pre-build breadth across every vertical, workflow, UI, tool, connector, and business process. The compounding loop is that each successful materialization cycle captures reusable capability residue — context, scaffold, UI shape, connector, verifier, memory, prompt pattern, taste preference, warning, or harness — so the system becomes broader through use. Praxis is hyperfocus as software: it lets a human focus intensely on the moment of work while the platform assembles the right data, shape, tools, context, agents, and verification, then distills what worked into the growing toolbox. This makes breadth a consequence of repeated focused work rather than a prerequisite for building the platform.
decisions.title: Breadth becomes an output, not an input; Praxis is hyperfocus as software

decisions.rationale: Core product/architecture doctrine: Human-facing AI collaboration should feel like chat-level flexibility backed by deterministic guardrails. Safety and business rules must become environmental constraints — read-only fields, scoped sandboxes, explicit approval gates, tool allowlists, write envelopes, verifier requirements, and auditable receipts — rather than conversational reminders the model is expected to remember. Example: for ERP updates, financial fields are read-only in the task environment unless explicit approval unlocks them; the model cannot accidentally edit them because the environment, tool schema, and write scope make the wrong move impossible. This is confidence infrastructure materialized: human judgment becomes reusable system behavior, reducing the need to repeat rules and making future agent work safer by construction.
decisions.title: Confidence infrastructure, materialized: rules should be environmental, not conversational

decisions.rationale: Praxis is LLM-first infrastructure. LLMs are smart; they mostly make mistakes because the environment gives them incomplete context, too many ambiguous choices, hidden state, and weak proof. The trust compiler is the engine: it compiles the right context, legal actions, typed state, tools, data pills, gates, receipts, and recovery paths AT THE MOMENT OF ACTION — making the right choice the easiest choice. Not framed as "a better workflow builder." Not only framed as "trust compiler for messy work." Correct framing: Praxis is LLM-first infrastructure powered by a trust compiler. North Star: an LLM enters cold and asks "what is the goal, what context matters, what fields exist, what tools consume them, what moves are legal, what proof is required, what is blocked, what changed before, and what should I do next?" — and Praxis answers from DB-backed authority, not vibes. The trust compiler turns fuzzy intent, partial state, source refs, repo code, UI, external systems, previous records, bugs, roadmap, workflow runs, receipts, and human judgment into an agent-operable packet: relevant context, legal next actions, typed contracts, data pills, tool bindings, gates, proof requirements, recovery paths, human decision points.
decisions.title: Praxis is LLM-first infrastructure powered by a trust compiler

decisions.rationale: The next wave is AI-centric: workflows should be built around LLM-readable authority, typed state, queryable outcomes, deterministic contracts, receipts, and legal next actions. Human UI, natural language, and workflow canvases are supervision and correction surfaces over that authority, not the primary source of truth and not the mental model the runtime is optimized around.
decisions.title: Praxis is LLM-first with humans as supervisors, not human-first workflow builders

decisions.rationale: Long-term Praxis client value comes from connecting to operational systems, introspecting schemas, sampling comparable records from claimed source-of-truth and downstream systems, and using values plus metadata such as source, update timestamps, ownership hints, hierarchy, flattening, and automation touchpoints to infer the real object/process truth model. This avoids brittle rule sprawl like manually declaring which system wins for every field unless X. The platform should produce a reusable, evidence-backed map of object truth, confidence, lineage, deltas, unknowns, and process/integration edges; unresolved ambiguity becomes typed gaps or targeted questions. Tests remain useful, but the stronger foundation is current state + SOPs + historical evidence + inferred truth contracts.
decisions.title: Object truth should be inferred from sampled cross-system evidence, not hand-authored field precedence sprawl

decisions.rationale: Cross-system object truth work must not leave schema parsing, metadata extraction, object version modeling, identity grouping, field comparison, hierarchy/flattening detection, freshness scoring, or lineage tracking to ad hoc LLM judgment. External records and schemas should be brought into Praxis as structured primitive object versions plus dedicated metadata/evidence tables, then compared through deterministic jobs and queryable tools. LLMs may decide, explain, classify ambiguity, propose mappings, and ask targeted questions only on top of this substrate, with confidence, evidence links, and typed gaps preserved. The platform should support both automatic runs and purpose-built operator tools for these jobs so object truth contracts are reproducible, inspectable, and reusable.
decisions.title: Object truth inference requires deterministic parse, metadata, version, and comparison substrate before LLM judgment

decisions.rationale: Anchor: product_architecture::llm-first-infrastructure-trust-compiler-engine. Five-phase plan over ~10 weeks delivering Praxis public beta as LLM-first infrastructure powered by a trust compiler. North Star: LLM enters cold and asks goal/context/fields/tools/legal-moves/proof/blocked/history/next-action; Praxis answers from DB-backed authority. PHASE 1 (weeks 1-3) LAUNCH COMPILER FOUNDATION: 1.1 LLM-first Launch Compiler (description-only PlanPacket, source_refs: [] resolve, data-dictionary binding, atomic compile_plan, typed gaps, derived packet_map with inferred_stage/resolved_agent/capabilities/write_envelope/expected_gates/verification_gaps, plan.launched event); 1.2 Built-not-wired sweep (wire derive_bug_packets, workflow_type_contracts, validate_workflow_request_type_flow, data_dictionary.intent_binding); 1.3 Data dictionary as universal compile-time clamp; 1.4 DataPill primitive family (10 tables + praxis_data_pill MCP); 1.5 Fail-closed part 1 (silent auto/stage + Python-only verify_refs become typed gaps); 1.6 Events plan.launched+typed_gap.created. PHASE 2 (weeks 3-5) MOON COMPOSER CLOSURE: 2.1 Moon commitDefinition uses same compiler; 2.2 Close BUG-C6EE740C/BUG-5DD67C2A/BUG-99B9DC7E/BUG-2729F8B7/BUG-FC232024; 2.3 Visual fixes BUG-EBFFD508/BUG-FA546993; 2.4 Events composer.compiled+graph.mutated; 2.5 Moon auto/build and suggest_next timeout become typed gaps + partial-state. PHASE 3 (weeks 5-7) AGENT OPERATING SUBSTRATE: 3.1 agent_context_packet with freshness; 3.2 Typed failure taxonomy + recovery edges; 3.3 Trust levels on state + external-edges; 3.4 Source-linked outcome graph MVP (source_artifacts/source_anchors/source_outcome_links, first path MoonActionDock.tsx); 3.5 Visual geometry authority (ui_layout_snapshots, layout_audit_findings, ui_audit primitive); 3.6 Events projection.reconciled+trust_drift.detected. PHASE 4 (weeks 7-9) HUMAN CONTROL ROOM + PATTERNS: 4.1 Supervision primitives (override, bless, promote, merge/split, approval gate UI); 4.2 workflow_pattern_packs + integration_builder_pack end-to-end; 4.3 graph_health_audit primitive; 4.4 Consolidation cleanup (merge parallel typing, retire surface_catalog_reviews/workflow_projection/shadow_execution_packet, extract ProjectionSubscriber base, project Moon families from operation_catalog). PHASE 5 (weeks 9-10) BETA RAMP: 5.1 End-to-end demo (integration-builder prompt -> typed graph -> legal actions -> gates -> typed gaps with repair -> discovery -> retrieval -> evaluation -> fallback -> promoted pack); 5.2 Docs + onboarding packs; 5.3 Smoke tests. OUTCOME GATE: three operators onboard via pack install, steer via Moon, ship one integration; graph_health_audit returns empty findings. BINDING STANDING ORDERS (operator_decisions.architecture_policy): one-graph-many-lenses, data-dictionary-universal-compile-time-clamp, source-refs-plural-canonical-shape, data-pill-primitive-family, expected-envelope-vs-actual-truth-separation, post-hoc-derivation-over-pre-declared-guess, fail-closed-at-compile-no-silent-defaults, llm_first_operator_model_human_supervision_surfaces, source-linked-outcome-graph, polymorphic-from (superseded by source_refs plural). FIRST STEP: Phase 1.1 LLM-first Launch Compiler lunchbox. Sub-steps (a-i): PlanPacket description-only, source_refs plural polymorphic resolution, compile-time dictionary binding, atomic compile_plan rollback, typed gap on no admitted route, post-run write_set derivation, derived packet_map fields, plan.launched event, unit tests. Pre-code: praxis workflow discover "launch_plan compile_plan PlanPacket"; read current code; scope smallest doctrine-proving change preserving old paths as migration. Roadmap program entry: roadmap_item.praxis.public.beta.ramp.llm.first.infrastructure.wedge (note: hard_cutover_program template auto-added 5 unrelated child items about roadmap-authoring cutover; those need cleanup separately — they do not represent the 5 phases above).
decisions.title: Praxis Public Beta Ramp — Master Plan (LLM-first Infrastructure Wedge)

decisions.rationale: A2A is now a first-class protocol surface for Praxis: agent identity, skills, tasks, messages, artifacts, task status, and agent interoperability should be represented cleanly through API, CQRS projections, and durable database identity. This does not reduce Praxis to a protocol implementation. Praxis keeps the larger operating model: durable work programs, memory, audit, authority, routing, operator control, decisions, blockers, artifacts, evidence, replay, and next-action state. A2A is the native wire and compatibility layer; Praxis remains the stateful control plane for agentic work.
decisions.title: Praxis is a stateful A2A server without shrinking its core

decisions.rationale: For this operator instance, LLM API capability must mechanically disappear from every job type except compile. The only allowed API route for compile is Together deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro. DeepSeek V3.2, Cursor API, OpenRouter API, and every non-compile API row must be unavailable in the effective provider job catalog; CLI lanes remain governed separately.
decisions.title: Private API lanes are compile-only through Together DeepSeek V4 Pro

decisions.rationale: Operator directive: turn off anthropic. Disable applies to every (transport, job_type, adapter, model) tuple via wildcard selector.
decisions.title: Anthropic disabled across all transports and job types

decisions.rationale: Operator directive: CLI/provider execution is the provider lane for every use case, always, unless the operator explicitly says otherwise. API is opt-in only; do not use API routes merely because a UI/app surface could use them or because CLI seems inconvenient. Every provider-routing discussion must label CLI versus API explicitly. Direct DeepSeek API remains research-only unless explicitly changed. DeepSeek through OpenRouter is still API and must be explicitly requested.
decisions.title: CLI is the provider execution lane for every use case unless explicitly overridden

decisions.rationale:
DeepSeek through the OpenRouter broker is reachable only via task_type='materialize', which is the Moon "Describe it" UI compile lane. No other task_type may resolve to a deepseek model.

Concretely:
- task_type='materialize' rank=1: openrouter/deepseek-v4-flash (preserved).
- task_type='materialize' rank=2: openrouter/deepseek-v4-pro  (preserved as compile fallback only).
- task_type='build' → deepseek-v4-pro: removed (was rank=1 explicit).
- task_type='build' → deepseek-v4-flash: removed (was rank=27 derived from cap_build_med).
- candidate.openrouter.deepseek-v4-pro: task_affinities narrowed to compile-only; build/coding/review/analysis/architecture/chat moved to "avoid"; cap_build_high / cap_build_med / cap_review / cap_tool_use / cap_analysis_architecture_research toggled off so capability matching cannot reroute it back into build work.
- candidate.openrouter.deepseek-v4-flash: cap_build_high / cap_build_med toggled off so derived task_type_routing cannot reintroduce a build row.
- Build now resolves through CLI candidates by default; the surviving API build candidates (sonnet-4.6, gpt-5.x, gemini-3.x, etc.) cover any required API fallback.

Untouched (out of scope for this decision):
- provider_slug='deepseek' direct API (model deepseek-r3) remains the research-only lane per feedback_deepseek_research_only.
- Sonnet 4.6 stays at task_type='build' rank=2 as the API build fallback.

Rationale: the operator's standing boundary is "API is opt-in, CLI is the default". The compile lane is an explicit API exception for the Describe-it UI surface. Allowing deepseek to resolve for build broadened that exception beyond the UI compile path. This decision restores the boundary: deepseek-via-OpenRouter is a UI-compile-only lane.

Rollback path: re-INSERT the build → openrouter/deepseek-v4-pro task_type_routing row at rank=1; restore v4-pro candidate task_affinities and cap_* flags from migration 245.
decisions.title: DeepSeek (via OpenRouter) is only reachable from the UI compile path

decisions.rationale: Operator explicitly authorized opening the OpenRouter API lane after DeepSeek V4-Pro via OpenRouter tested successfully but the compile resolver rejected it with lane.rejected.payg_fallback_disabled. This decision opens provider_slug=openrouter adapter_type=llm_task by setting allow_payg_fallback=true. Scope is API/OpenRouter only. CLI remains the default provider execution lane for every use case unless explicitly overridden.
decisions.title: OpenRouter API paid lane is open when explicitly routed

decisions.rationale: Provider/model routing must not hide reasoning effort inside model slugs, one-off prompt text, or provider-specific extra_body blobs. Praxis should maintain a separate effort matrix keyed by provider, model, transport lane, task type, and internal effort slug, then compile that into provider-specific payload controls such as OpenAI reasoning effort, Anthropic thinking controls, OpenRouter reasoning, or CLI flags. Route choice, cost, latency, capacity, fallback, and receipts must all see the selected effort level explicitly.
decisions.title: Reasoning effort is a first-class provider routing dimension

decisions.rationale: A missing runtime_profile_ref means broad catalog routing only. Runtime-profile admission may be used only when a caller passes an explicit runtime_profile_ref or intentionally opts into default runtime-profile lookup through a named API. Execution paths must fail closed when unresolved auto routes reach execution without scoped route authority; they must not silently recover by broad-routing against the global provider catalog.
decisions.title: Runtime-profile admission is explicit, not a router default

decisions.rationale: Claude Sonnet 4.7 is not a valid model slug in this Praxis registry. Opus may advance independently; Sonnet remains claude-sonnet-4-6 until a registry-backed migration or provider authority row promotes a real successor. Runtime profiles, examples, and probes must not infer Sonnet versions from Opus versions.
decisions.title: Do not synthesize Claude Sonnet 4.7

decisions.rationale:
DeepSeek is an API model accessed directly (not through OpenRouter). Provider slug is `deepseek`, endpoint is `https://api.deepseek.com/v1/chat/completions`, protocol is `openai_chat_completions`.

**Why:** User corrected assumption that it was CLI or via OpenRouter. It's a direct API integration using the OpenAI-compatible protocol.

**How to apply:** When referencing DeepSeek in specs, use `deepseek/deepseek-r3` (not `openrouter/deepseek/...`). Transport is remote/API pool, not local/CLI pool.

decisions.rationale: Operator capacity: Cursor is highest-capacity agent lane available. Onboard cursor as CLI transport (composer-2) and enable for all non-research job_types so the workflow runtime can route build/code/refactor work through it. Research stays on Gemini-2.5 lanes per existing exception.
decisions.title: Cursor enabled as CLI provider for non-research job types

decisions.rationale: Cursor has no usable lane right now: API path is Background-Agent-only (cloud sandbox + git-pull, architectural mismatch with Praxis local-sandbox + receipts model). CLI lane works at the wizard probe layer (cursor-agent binary returns) but workflow runtime sandbox dispatch fails before cursor-agent is ever invoked. Net effect: every route is admittance-without-execution. Disable everywhere; revisit only when (a) Cursor exposes a non-Background-Agent API, OR (b) the workflow sandbox dispatch lane for CLI providers is unblocked.
decisions.title: Cursor scrubbed across all transports and job types

decisions.rationale:
DeepSeek has exactly two permitted lanes. Never let it leak into anything else.

1. **Direct DeepSeek API** (`provider_slug='deepseek'`, model `deepseek/deepseek-r3`) — research-only containment. Never picked by the router for build, architecture, debate, review, or any general-purpose `auto/` lane.
2. **DeepSeek via the OpenRouter broker** (`provider_slug='openrouter'`, model `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` or `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`) — UI compile only (`task_type='materialize'`, the Moon "Describe it" path). Never `task_type='build'`, `chat`, `review`, `analysis`, or general operator CLI/MCP work.

**Why:** Operator boundary "API is opt-in, CLI is the default" + "we are only using DeepSeek for compile from the UI and not any other time" (2026-04-25). The compile lane is the single authorized API exception. Earlier migrations (243, 245) had let DeepSeek-via-OpenRouter resolve for `task_type='build'` rank=1; migration 246 closed that leak.

**How to apply:**
- Never hardcode `deepseek/deepseek-r3` in workflow specs — even on research workflows. Use `auto/research` and let the router decide.
- Never add a `task_type_routing` row that maps any non-`compile` task_type to a deepseek model under provider `openrouter`. The only legitimate compile callers are `runtime/compiler.py` → `runtime/compiler_llm.py:call_llm_compile`, the Moon "Describe it" surface.
- For the v4-pro candidate, "build", "coding", "review", "analysis" etc. live in `task_affinities.avoid` — keep them there; do not re-add them to `primary`/`secondary` without operator sign-off.
- If a new caller needs an API LLM lane, route it through a non-deepseek model (e.g., sonnet at task_type='build' rank=2) unless the operator explicitly expands DeepSeek scope.
decisions.title: DeepSeek has exactly two permitted lanes. Never let it leak into anything else.

decisions.rationale: Operator directive: any new (provider, transport, job_type, adapter, model) route added to the access matrix lands DENIED by default. Operator must explicitly flip ON via praxis_access_control action=enable, anchored to a decision row, before the route is dispatchable. Reverses the historic default-admit posture which let new model variants leak into routing without explicit operator approval. Applies retroactively for any new onboarding from 2026-04-27 forward.
decisions.title: New provider/model route additions default to OFF

decisions.rationale:
Never route Gemini 2.5 (any variant — `gemini-2.5-flash`, `gemini-2.5-pro`, `gemini-2.5-flash-lite`, etc.) to building, code changes, analysis, recon, closeout, or anything load-bearing. Use `gemini-3.1-pro-preview` (or newer pro-tier) for serious Gemini work; older 2.5 only for "bullshit" / throwaway jobs.

**Why:** the operator says "the router is clear on 2.5-flash for a reason" and explicitly extended that to "no Gemini 2.5 ever for building or anything besides bullshit jobs." Quality-tier policy, not capability gap.

**How to apply:** when authoring queue specs, debate routes, integration helpers, or any agent assignment that nominates Google/Gemini, pick a 3.x model (e.g. `google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview`, `google/gemini-3-flash-preview`). If the CLI provider probe only discovers `gemini-2.5-flash`, that is a discovery gap — the live CLI accepts 3.x models when authenticated. Override with `adapter_type: cli_llm` and the desired 3.x model rather than downgrading to 2.5.
decisions.title: Never route Gemini 2.5 (any variant — `gemini-2.5-flash`, `gemini-2.5-pro`, `gemini-2.5-flash-lite`, etc.) to building,

decisions.rationale:
OpenRouter may only be invoked from the Praxis app user surfaces — the Moon
chat surface and the "Describe it" compile button. It must NEVER be bound
into the workflow runtime_profile (e.g. `praxis`) where agent runs, sandbox
tasks, or background jobs could hit it.

**Why:** OpenRouter is a metered-per-call API. Local CLI providers (Claude
Code, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI) are free for Nate's primary operator use.
Routing background workflow jobs through OpenRouter would turn every
unattended run into a paid call.

**How to apply:**
- Chat surface: OpenRouter is pinned via `task_type_routing` explicit rows
  (rank=1 for chat/build/review/analysis) — this is fine because the chat
  orchestrator calls `resolve_failover_chain("auto/chat")` without a
  runtime_profile_ref. Only the app chat triggers this path.
- "Describe it" compile: bypass workflow-job submission entirely. The
  compile path in `runtime/compiler_llm.py::call_llm_compile` hits
  OpenRouter directly via `adapters.llm_client.call_llm`, using the
  Keychain-resolved `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`. No `submit_workflow_inline`,
  no `runtime_profile_ref='praxis'`.
- If a future feature needs OpenRouter-style paid calls, reach for the
  same direct-HTTP pattern rather than binding OpenRouter into a runtime
  profile or creating a new profile that admits it.
decisions.title: OpenRouter may only be invoked from the Praxis app user surfaces — the Moon

decisions.rationale:
When Nate names a specific provider — "Together.ai", "OpenRouter", "DeepSeek direct" — use exactly that. Do not substitute a different provider just because:
- The other provider's key happens to already be in env/keychain
- The other provider already has working wiring in the codebase
- The other provider has the same model available

**Why:** Today (2026-04-25) Nate said "Together.ai" for the operator-console V4 Pro chat. I went to OpenRouter because the OPENROUTER_API_KEY was loaded and the model was registered there. He had to repeat himself multiple times, with escalating frustration, before I switched. That cost real time and trust during a session where he had to leave urgently.

**How to apply:** When the user names a provider, search for that provider's key (try multiple service-name variants in keychain — `TOGETHER_API_KEY`, `together`, etc.) before falling back to anything else. If the key isn't found, ask once where it is rather than silently routing through a different provider.

**Reinforced 2026-04-26:** Repeated the mistake. UI compile was failing because the LLM call needed an API key. I tried wiring the existing OpenRouter→DeepSeek route from migrations 243+246 instead of asking which provider to use. Nate corrected: "use Together, you have the API in the keychain, but it's all API not CLI." Together V4-Pro is already registered in `provider_model_candidates` (priority 500) and `provider_endpoint_bindings` resolves it to `https://api.together.xyz/v1/chat/completions` with `TOGETHER_API_KEY` env. The compile route just needs `task_type_routing` swapped to point at it. Pattern: when *any* LLM call fails for missing-key reasons, list the provider candidates the user has in keychain *first* before assuming the registered route is the one they want.

**Reinforced again 2026-04-26 (same session):** Stacked migrations 262/264/265 to wire Together as the compile + plan_section_author primary, then Nate corrected: "We use the together AI API and it was actually already implimented like three times im not sure why you changed it again." Migrations 251 (`register_together_direct_provider`) and 252 (`plan_section_author_together_primary`) already had Together wired. I should have searched task_type_routing + provider_endpoint_bindings + provider_model_candidates BEFORE writing 262/264/265 — would have found Together already at the right rank. **Pattern:** when about to write a migration that promotes/registers a provider, first run `SELECT * FROM task_type_routing WHERE provider_slug='<provider>'` and read the existing migrations matching `*_<provider>_*.sql`. If a recent migration already does what you're about to do, change the data via UPDATE in a small follow-up rather than re-registering from scratch. Stacked redundant migrations are mostly harmless but they cost trust and review cycles.
decisions.title: When Nate names a specific provider — "Together.ai", "OpenRouter", "DeepSeek direct" — use exactly that. Do not substitu

decisions.rationale: Public docs and surfaces should use Praxis for the product, Praxis Engine for the runtime, Praxis.db for durable authority, workflow for executable work, and launch for starting the system.
decisions.title: Public vocabulary uses Praxis, Praxis Engine, Praxis.db, workflow, and launch

decisions.rationale: Router has per-provider concurrency + load-balancing: rate_limiter.py::ProviderThrottleRegistry, execution_backends.py::provider_slot_bypass, task_type_router.attach_route_plans (failover chains, round-robin rotation). "unresolved_routes" / "task_type_router could not resolve auto/* slug" lives at admission.py:1132-1148 — resolver layer, upstream of the limiter. Don't propose limiter changes for resolver bugs.
decisions.title: Resolver ≠ rate-limiter — distinct layers

decisions.rationale:
Never put capability-class terms like `"image"`, `"image-generation"`, `"image-editing"` in a task type profile's `affinity_labels.avoid` list.

**Why:** `_match_affinity_bucket` in `task_type_router.py` checks whether the candidate's positive labels (capability_tags + task_affinities primary/secondary) intersect with the profile's avoid list. Putting `"image"` in the profile avoid is intended to exclude image-generation models — but text models also declare `"image"` in their own `task_affinities.avoid` (meaning "I don't do image work"). These were two different uses of the same label. The old code checked `avoid_labels ∩ profile_avoid`, which caused every text model to be classified as "avoid" → zero candidates → `No permitted models for task type 'build'`. Fixed in code (line 1097) to check positive_labels instead, and DB was patched to remove image terms from all 10 profiles.

**How to apply:** Profile avoid lists should only contain labels that would appear in a specialized model's *positive* labels (e.g. a dedicated TTS model would have "tts" in its capability_tags). Never use generic capability-gap declarations here.
decisions.title: Never put capability-class terms like `"image"`, `"image-generation"`, `"image-editing"` in a task type profile's `affin

decisions.rationale: execution_backends.py:97 _sanitize_base_env and docker_runner.py:155 strip list already drop CLAUDECODE correctly. Don't propose changes there to "fix" Claude Code agent shell pollution. Fix at agent's tool-permission layer instead (Bash deny on `praxis:*`).
decisions.title: Don't touch CLAUDECODE handling in sandbox

decisions.rationale: Shim stdlib needs ≠ agent's needs. Agent runs pytest/scripts/migrations on Praxis code → needs same interpreter as API server. praxis-claude/codex/gemini Dockerfiles base off python:3.14-slim + install Node 22 on top. Future sandbox image work must keep sandbox Python in lockstep with API-server Python — `python3 --version` returning anything ≠ working sandbox.
decisions.title: Sandbox Python matches platform Python (3.14)

decisions.rationale: surfaces.api.rest._create_capability_endpoint must merge inputs so that path_params (server-routed from URL templates) always win over query_params, which always win over JSON body fields. Server-routed values like run_id from /v1/runs/{run_id}:cancel must never be shadowed by attacker-controlled body or query input. When the bound command class declares a 'body' field, the entire JSON body is passed through unflattened; path/query still override at the top level. Body must be a JSON object — bare arrays/scalars are rejected with 400. Resolves BUG-B3315290 trust-boundary gap. Locked by regression test test_capability_endpoint_path_params_cannot_be_shadowed_by_body in tests/unit/test_operation_catalog_mounting.py.
decisions.title: REST capability endpoints enforce path > query > body precedence

decisions.rationale: Validators, equivalence checks, causal propagations (cache invalidation, event cascades), retraction cascades, temporal validity, and trust weighting all live as typed rows in semantic_predicate_catalog.  Each row declares applies_to_kind, predicate_kind, optional validator_ref, and a propagation_policy JSONB.  The catalog is itself catalog-mounted so record/list/get reach REST/MCP/CLI without bespoke wiring.  Effects: one declaration replaces N hand-written validators/subscribers; bypass detection becomes meaningful (did the write satisfy declared invariants and fire implied propagations?), not just structural; the meaning layer becomes queryable.
decisions.title: Semantic predicates are DB-declared and auto-propagate to every surface

decisions.rationale: Fresh operators may start with none of the required platform dependencies. The setup frontdoor must fail fast with an explicit blocker list and exact install guidance for required prerequisites such as Python, Postgres, pgvector, psql, Docker, and Node when relevant. It should separate required core prerequisites from optional provider credentials, demos, and UI extras. Praxis may support multiple Postgres deployment topologies, but it should not pretend to support arbitrary database engines or silently install host-level dependencies across package managers.
decisions.title: Setup frontdoor is prerequisite-first, not dependency-installer

decisions.rationale: Praxis should not treat bugs, roadmap items, UI geometry, source code, integrations, outside apps, receipts, and vector or retrieval stores as separate registries glued together by prose. They are vertices in one source-linked outcome graph, joined by typed edges such as implements, renders, owns, blocks, validates, depends_on, observed_in, imports_from, syncs_to, retrieves_from, and supersedes. External apps and vector databases can appear at the graph boundary as integration or memory vertices, but authority must remain explicit: DB rows define durable relationships, receipts prove observations, and embeddings support retrieval without becoming the source of truth.
decisions.title: Outcome graph links code, UI, bugs, roadmap, integrations, receipts, and external memory by typed edges

decisions.rationale: Praxis should make repository code and runtime outcomes semantically linked. Source files, components, routes, tools, schemas, workflows, UI elements, geometry snapshots, gates, receipts, and audit findings need stable source anchors so an LLM can understand both the script side and the outcome side of a system. Code edits should not directly hand-write geometry or outcome rows; they should trigger deterministic projections and verification receipts that update source-linked read models. This gives future LLM runs a queryable map from code changes to UI geometry, backend contracts, runtime behavior, and release risk.
decisions.title: Repo code changes must compile to source-linked outcome projections for UI and backend surfaces

decisions.rationale: Structured-document retrieval must extend Praxis CQRS, data-dictionary, service-bus, and semantic assertion authority. Section hierarchy identifies where evidence lives; semantic assertions define what it means; embeddings and vector similarity are recall projections only. Context assembly must write receipts for selected sections and must not create a parallel context compiler or unqueryable RAG store.
decisions.title: Structured document context is semantic authority, not standalone RAG

decisions.rationale: submission_required jobs need tangible evidence: real diff in write_scope (code-seal) OR a deliverable file written to write_scope (written-seal). "Agent ran cleanly" is not "agent did the work." Don't synthesize empty submissions in submission_gate to paper over no-diff jobs.
decisions.title: stdout-only ≠ submission

decisions.rationale:
Workflow specs now support `verify_command` and `outcome_goal` on each job. After the agent exits and the submission gate passes, the worker runs `verify_command` as a shell command (cwd=repo_root, timeout=60s). Non-zero exit = job fails with `outcome_gate_failed`.

**Why:** Agents were "succeeding" with zero work — exiting cleanly without implementing the spec. Auto-seal made it worse by sealing empty output. The outcome gate catches this by grep-checking for expected code, running tsc, etc.

**How to apply:** Every build sprint should have a `verify_command` that proves the deliverable exists. Example:
```json
{
  "verify_command": "grep -q 'export function extractLayout' 'path/to/file.ts'",
  "outcome_goal": "extractLayout function exists in the presenter"
}
```

Implementation: `_execution_core.py` runs the gate after submission_gate, before complete_job. Reads `verify_command` from the spec snapshot.
decisions.title: Workflow specs now support `verify_command` and `outcome_goal` on each job. After the agent exits and the submission gat

decisions.rationale: Moon, Atlas, Dashboard, and quadrant-manifest workspaces today read data through useModuleData(endpoint: string) — hand-rolled REST handlers addressed by a free-text path (platform-overview, observability/bug-scoreboard, runs/recent, bugs). There is no typed binding from a module quadrant to a data-dictionary pill, a workflow_run outcome, a consumes/produces type, a freshness policy, or an event stream. Module catalog itself is fragmented across three non-overlapping registries (registry_ui_components migration 016 carries props_schema + emits_events + accepts_slots metadata that is never read at runtime, blocks/catalog.v1.json, and the JS moduleRegistry.ts — none share IDs), seed bundles are hard-coded in seedBundles.ts instead of app_manifests rows, shell tabs are hard-coded in surfaceRegistry.tsx though ui_shell_route_registry (217) already mirrors them, and ui_feature_flow_registry (218) describes feature→tool→workflow→primitive chains the React code does not read. Pairs with and binds this direction to three already-filed policies: (1) architecture-policy::platform-architecture::conceptual-events-register-through-operation-catalog-registry — every write surface routes through operation_catalog_registry (command side) + authority_event_contracts (observation side); parallel emission paths are rejected. (2) architecture-policy::platform-architecture::data-dictionary-universal-compile-time-clamp — data dictionary is the universal type authority; do not build parallel typing stacks. (3) architecture-policy::platform-architecture::legal-action-graph-is-required-compiler-output — compiler must narrow the palette to legal continuations. Decision: (a) all module read paths must be typed projections registered in authority_projection_registry + authority_projection_contracts (migration 204 substrate); (b) module config.source replaces config.endpoint as the binding point, keyed by projection_ref (FK to authority_projection_registry.projection_ref); (c) projection envelope returned to the client carries last_event_id, last_receipt_id, last_refreshed_at, freshness_status from authority_projection_state so freshness is auditable client-side; (d) NO parallel module_projection_registry table — that would violate the conceptual-events-through-OCR policy; (e) legal_modules(state), surface_for(workflow_run), and other surface-composition read models are implemented as additional authority_projection_contracts rows, not as new registries; (f) command side (add module, bind pill, launch workflow from surface, approve gate) continues to route through operation_catalog_registry; (g) scope is read-side only — the legality compiler that narrows the palette by consuming pills and gates is downstream of Phase 1.4 DataPill / PillBinding work and typed_gap primitive. Lunchbox v1 for this direction migrates exactly one preset (pass-rate) to the projection path as proof.
decisions.title: Surface workspace composition routes through typed CQRS projections, not raw REST endpoint strings

decisions.rationale: Experience templates consume pill types from a DAG lattice (data dictionary subtype_of edges) with typed slots; growth is through new subtypes, not new shapes; specialized templates beat generic ones via lattice-depth specificity ranking, with generic fallback required for graceful degradation. Risk mitigation reuses existing authority: (a) praxis_data_dictionary_drift + praxis_data_dictionary_wiring_audit for lattice pollution and template-field access violations; (b) praxis_data_dictionary_lineage + praxis_data_dictionary_impact for field inheritance change propagation; (c) task_type_routing shape mirrored as intent_template_routing — same rank/fallback/audit posture, no new substrate; (d) pgvector + praxis_discover for retrieval suggestions ONLY, never for admission; admission is always through typed graph walk through legal_templates projection; (e) authority_event_contracts (migration 204) for replayable template.registered / template.bound / template.used / theme_token.changed events; (f) projection.reconciled events for palette freshness without polling; (g) typed_gap.created event contract (migration 226) for no-match failures with repair_actions, auto-promoting severe or recurring gaps to praxis_bugs; (h) praxis_data_dictionary_classifications + praxis_data_dictionary_governance for PII/sensitivity tag cascade through slot renderers; (i) authority_event_projection_contract_report for projection-contract well-formedness at migration time; (j) authority_operation_receipts for usage_decay signal in the specificity ranker (template.last_used_at comes free); (k) praxis_semantic_assertions for template contract claims that reconcile can flag; (l) ui_feature_flow_registry primitive_refs extended to carry template refs for feature-to-surface traceability. Specificity ranker is graph-deterministic: rank = specificity_depth × intent_binding_weight × usage_decay, each signal grounded in existing authority. Write-path gate surface.template.register MUST validate before commit: (1) every consumes type exists in data_dictionary_objects, (2) every emits operation exists in operation_catalog_registry, (3) every field referenced is granted by declared consumes via subtype inheritance, (4) ambiguous same-specificity bindings rejected unless explicit priority tie-breaker set, (5) specialized templates (consumes past base types) require explicit fallback_ref or a generic sibling. Fail-closed per the conceptual-events-through-OCR policy. Parallel risk-mitigation plumbing (ad-hoc template registries outside authority_projection_contracts, inline template-dispatch logic, string-key field access past slot types, embedding-based admission gates) is rejected. Pairs with architecture-policy::surface-catalog::surface-composition-cqrs-direction (filed same day) and architecture-policy::platform-architecture::legal-is-computable-not-permitted (filed same day).
decisions.title: Experience-template risk mitigation reuses existing Praxis authority surfaces; no parallel plumbing

decisions.rationale: Both go to operation_catalog_gateway. CLI: argv → gateway. MCP: JSON-RPC → gateway. Audience split: agents → MCP; operators-in-terminal + scripts/launchd → CLI. Sandbox shim is a separate thin POST-to-MCP client, not the full CLI. Feature work lands in both surfaces unless a decision narrows audience.
decisions.title: CLI and MCP are siblings, neither wraps the other

decisions.rationale: When a low-level helper catches Exception and re-raises as a single typed error with a single reason_code, it destroys the operator-actionable signal. ensure_postgres_available was the canonical example: it caught EVERY exception during _bootstrap() (connectivity, schema-readiness, migration-policy drift, RuntimeError on incomplete bootstrap) and re-raised them all as PostgresConfigurationError with reason_code="postgres.authority_unavailable". Upstream wrappers then layered "native runtime authority unavailable: postgres.authority_unavailable" on top, so the operator saw a database-connectivity error when the actual cause was a registered-migration drift the operator had to fix in the system_authority JSON. The same thing was happening at submission_gate Stage 4 (verification.required_not_run regardless of why verification didn't run) and at refresh_private_provider_job_catalog (cardinality violation surfaced as opaque schema_bootstrap_failed). Rule, binding on every future build packet: when a wrapper transforms an inner exception, it must inspect the inner type and pick a reason_code that names the actual fault class, not the wrapper's own concern. The wrapping layer can still standardize the error TYPE (PostgresConfigurationError, NativeRuntimeProfileSyncError, etc.) but the reason_code must remain heterogeneous. Reference fix: Code&DBs/Workflow/storage/postgres/connection.py:846-882 maps WorkflowMigrationError → workflow.migration_policy_drift with the canonical remediation string; RuntimeError("schema bootstrap incomplete") → workflow.schema_bootstrap_incomplete; only genuine connectivity errors keep postgres.authority_unavailable. Pairs with the existing standing order on operator-tool routing — operators ACT on the reason_code, so the reason_code must be actionable.
decisions.title: Exception wrappers must surface the actual cause class, not collapse heterogeneous failures into a generic reason_code

decisions.rationale: No table accepting INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE may have writers in BOTH the runtime/ AND surfaces/ trees. The runtime layer owns durable state machines and authority modules; the surfaces layer is parser+renderer. When a surface needs to mutate a domain table it must call into the authority module that owns the table, never inline its own SQL. After the cross-layer audit in this thread, zero tables remain split. Repository helpers in storage/postgres/*_repository.py are the recommended seam for surface-level helpers that need to compose mutations with their domain logic. Rationale: cross-layer split duplicates invariant enforcement, breaks the CQRS contract, and makes domain rules hard to evolve. Pre-existing single-authority modules (operator_write, agent_sessions interactive lane, integration_registry) remain valid — they ARE the authority for their domain even though they live under surfaces/api/.
decisions.title: Domain tables must be authored from one layer (runtime XOR surfaces), never both

decisions.rationale: Tests that exercise bug authority writes must run inside explicit rollback isolation. Durable test rows pollute operator queues and turn test fixtures into false production state.
decisions.title: Bug-surface test writes require rollback isolation

decisions.rationale: Verify praxis_operator_architecture_policy unblocks after migration 238 backfilled architecture_policy predicate allowlists. This row is intentionally a smoke-test artifact and may be retired.
decisions.title: Smoke test BUG-C0E701DC fix

decisions.rationale: Praxis should treat UI layout, visibility, overlap, hierarchy, and interaction reachability as queryable product state, not as screenshot-only human judgment. Moon and future UI builders must persist or expose rendered geometry, component hierarchy, layout collisions, viewport reachability, text-fit metrics, and visual audit receipts so LLM customers can decompose, understand, critique, and build UI from authoritative visual facts. Screenshots remain evidence artifacts, but geometry and audit receipts are the machine-readable authority.
decisions.title: UI decomposition, understanding, and building require first-class visual geometry authority

decisions.rationale: LLM-facing UI maps must load surface source-file anchors from ui_surface_file_anchor_registry instead of Python or React constants so future agents can query one DB-backed authority before changing renderer files.
decisions.title: UI experience graph source-file anchors are registry-owned

decisions.rationale: Model sandboxes must not receive full repo copies by default and must not fall back to praxis-worker:latest. Runtime targets are substrate-neutral authority rows operated through API/MCP; CLI and website are clients, and SSH is only build/deploy transport for selected targets. Provider-family thin images provide the baked praxis tool, MCP context access, and sealed submission path back to Praxis.db.
decisions.title: Default model execution uses empty thin provider sandboxes

decisions.rationale: Operator surfaces must not treat green-looking state as truth by itself. Queue depth, running status, or a successful control-command response must be cross-checked against independent runtime evidence before declaring progress: container/process activity, fresh heartbeat, receipt timeline, sealed submission, artifact output, or verifier evidence. A control surface that reports success while stale authority prevents execution is more dangerous than an explicit failure.
decisions.title: Green-looking workflow state requires independent proof

decisions.rationale: A manifest is the moment-in-time execution packet, but the job contract inside it is the pre-execution contract: the desired pattern, forbidden anti-patterns, allowed mutations, forbidden mutations, proof requirements, and stop/recontract conditions. LLMs may propose actions, but gateway/runtime/DB/sandbox enforcement must validate proposed mutations against the job contract before execution; receipts prove allowed or blocked attempts, they do not compensate for after-the-fact punishment.
decisions.title: Manifest carries an enforceable job contract

decisions.rationale: Token usage belongs in observability receipts after execution, not as pre-launch budget projections, CQRS approval gates, or tool surfaces that can refuse platform work. Token-budget gating is anti-platform because it substitutes a guessed resource estimate for operator intent and provider/runtime authority. Remove token-budget projection and launch refusal paths; keep actual token telemetry only as observed execution data.

does_not_apply_to: Observed token usage telemetry on completed execution receipts
Context-window packing required to fit provider model limits

decisions.title: Token budgets are not workflow execution authority

decisions.rationale: Workflow fleet operations must be gated by one successful proof path before broad launch or retry. Do not mass retry, fan out, or restart a fleet until one representative job has proven the current runtime path end to end or failed with a new explicit root cause. This keeps retries diagnostic instead of duplicative and prevents queue poison from being amplified.
decisions.title: One proof before fleet execution

decisions.rationale: When Nate supplies a high-value operating truth during failure recovery, the platform should convert it into durable authority, enforcement, or observability instead of relying on future chat memory. The system should learn operational control from intervention moments: one explicit contract, one queryable record, one verifiable behavior change.
decisions.title: Operator interventions should become durable runtime improvements

decisions.rationale: A retry is an operator control action, not a duplicate launch. Every workflow.retry command must carry the receipt-backed previous failure being retried and the material difference in the next attempt, so stale replay, blind button-mashing, and repeated unchanged failures are visible and rejectable.
decisions.title: Workflow retries must declare prior failure and retry delta

decisions.rationale: Workflow state labels such as submitted, queued, requeued, or running are not sufficient proof that work is executing. A workflow is only considered fired when runtime observability shows actual work: a claimed job with fresh heartbeat, a sandbox container or provider process, and progressing durable evidence such as receipts, submissions, artifacts, or output. If nothing is building, nothing is firing.
decisions.title: Submitted is not fired until observable work exists

decisions.rationale: Workflow model sandboxes receive scoped authority through signed workflow MCP tokens, not ambient API or DB access. Tokens carry allowed_tools plus source_refs and access_policy shard fields such as resolved_read_scope, declared_read_scope, write_scope, test_scope, and blast_radius. MCP/API tools must clamp results to that shard when they can prove native enforcement; broad router tools that cannot prove shard enforcement fail closed for scoped workflow sessions until result-level clamps exist. Repo materialization follows the same model: empty by default, scoped shard copy allowed, unscoped legacy copy requires explicit operator debug opt-in.
decisions.title: Workflow MCP tokens carry and enforce task shard authority

decisions.rationale: Praxis workflow surfaces must not maintain separate UI, spec, NL, and runtime graphs that require shims to keep aligned. Moon, natural-language composition, spec export/import, validation, execution, and run overlays are lenses or interpreters over the same Praxis.db-backed typed graph authority. Specs remain portable export/import artifacts, not the authoritative source of workflow behavior. Typed consumes/produces edges and gates must live in durable graph/catalog authority so LLM planning and Moon authoring can narrow next steps to legal continuations.
decisions.title: Workflow authoring, NL composition, execution, and run views share one DB-backed typed graph authority

decisions.rationale: Workflow intent binding must not fail silently when prose lacks explicit object.field refs. The data dictionary is the shared vocabulary authority, so loose or out-of-order prose should be matched against dictionary object and field text using deterministic synonym expansion and returned as suggested candidates. Exact object_kind.field_path binding remains the hard confirmation gate; suggestions are advisory until confirmed.
decisions.title: Loose prose must surface data-pill suggestions before exact binding

decisions.rationale: Operators must not manually craft schema_migrations rows for live migration promotion. The authoritative path should compute content_sha256 and bootstrap_role from canonical migration authority, verify target DB identity and expected-object readiness, write schema_migrations through a typed helper, and emit append-only authority operation receipt evidence so promotion state remains inspectable and auditable.
decisions.title: Live migration promotion must use typed CLI authority

decisions.rationale: The term compile is overloaded across intent interpretation, workflow plan generation, artifact caching, execution packet persistence, and ordinary code compilation. Operator-facing surfaces should describe the workflow-building step as draft plan, generate plan, materialize plan, or create execution plan. Reserve compile for low-level deterministic transforms where the analogy is exact, and keep compatibility aliases until callers migrate.
decisions.title: Operator-facing workflow creation should use plan-generation language instead of compile

decisions.rationale: Use materialize when Praxis turns a draft, candidate, projection, generated plan, UI surface description, SOP intake, or derived read model into concrete durable authority with receipts or persisted state. Do not use materialize for ordinary preview, rendering, parsing, model calls, workflow launch, or file generation that does not create authority. This preserves a useful operator/UI verb for Materialize Plan, Materialize Surface, Materialize Contract, and similar future surfaces.
decisions.title: Materialize is the reserved verb for turning proposed shapes into durable authority

decisions.rationale: Praxis workers must not carry host-specific fixed parallelism defaults in compose or local env. The worker runtime derives local slots from visible CPU and available RAM, while PRAXIS_WORKER_MAX_PARALLEL and PRAXIS_WORKFLOW_MAX_CONCURRENT_NODES remain explicit operator override caps.
decisions.title: Worker concurrency is resource-derived by default

decisions.rationale: For workspace_ref=praxis and host_ref=default, launcher resolution comes from registry workspace authority plus the PRAXIS_WORKSPACE_BASE_PATH environment, not a hardcoded mounted Windows path. On this Mac cutover the launcher environment resolves to /Users/nate/Praxis. The mounted Windows view and SSH path remain rollback-only sources and must not be treated as active primary workspace authority.
decisions.title: Launcher workspace authority is env-driven local workspace; Windows mount is retired

decisions.rationale: Service lifecycle CQRS must not encode Mac, Windows, or Linux as the authority boundary. Runtime targets may be browser, SaaS, managed cloud service, connector, LAN node, Mac mini home box, Windows machine, Linux host, or another reconciler-backed substrate. Desired state, events, projections, health contracts, and capability grants must target abstract runtime_target_ref/substrate_kind records so future operators and agents do not hardcode local OS path or process assumptions.
decisions.title: Service lifecycle authority must be runtime-target neutral

decisions.rationale:
The Praxis app standardizes on OpenRouter as THE chat/compile engine for the primary user. Concretely:

- Chosen model: `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6` via OpenRouter. Strong tool calling, structured output, consistent agent behavior — matches the requirements of the app chat surface and the "Describe it" compile button.
- OpenRouter credential lives in macOS Keychain under service="praxis", account="OPENROUTER_API_KEY". The runtime probe in `runtime/health.py::_provider_api_key_present` reads Keychain via `adapters.keychain.resolve_secret`.
- `provider_model_candidates` row `candidate.openrouter.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-6` is priority=1, route_tier=medium, latency_class=instant, task_affinities.primary covers chat/analysis/build/review/tool-use/agentic-coding.
- Explicit `task_type_routing` rows (rank=1) pin OpenRouter for `chat`, `build`, `review`, `analysis` — the four task types the app surfaces hit.
- Prior rank=1 anthropic/sonnet-4-5 chat row is bumped to rank=11 so the OpenRouter row wins.
- `provider_transport_admissions` for openrouter/llm_task is re-admitted; prior admission_reason "credential or endpoint missing" was a stale health probe result from before the Keychain secret was stored.

Scope: this decision governs the app's chat surface and the compile entry points ("Describe it" in `MoonBuildPage.tsx` and `Dashboard.tsx`). New/external users of the platform remain free to pick any provider — this is specifically about the default engine for the primary operator.
decisions.title: OpenRouter is the primary chat/compile engine for the Praxis app (Nate's primary user experience)

decisions.rationale: Nate has no direct Anthropic API key. The claude binary authenticates via OAuth (CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN) under the subscription plan. Direct calls to api.anthropic.com via ANTHROPIC_API_KEY are forbidden. Migration 181 restores the CLI profile + lane_policy (cli_llm-only) + concurrency rows that migration 180 over-zealously removed, but strips api_endpoint / api_key_env_vars from the CLI profile so the DB row itself can never be used to reach the HTTP API. Runtime code paths that tried to call api.anthropic.com directly (task_assembler._call_haiku, provider_onboarding anthropic_models_list probe, docker_runner ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env forwarding, provider_transport builtin fallback api_endpoint) are removed or neutralized in the same change.
decisions.title: Anthropic access is CLI-only (subscription, no API key)

decisions.rationale: Audit a2_secret_audit reviewed adapters/keychain.py, adapters/credentials.py, adapters/oauth_lifecycle.py, registry/provider_execution_registry.py, runtime/integrations/connector_executor.py, runtime/workflow/execution_backends.py, and memory/ingest.py. Keychain-backed paths include resolve_secret usage in adapters.credentials and workflow execution env assembly. Non-keychain paths remain in OAuth token storage reads, OAuth client_id/client_secret direct os.environ lookups, .env fallback reads, broad os.environ copying, and provider registry credential metadata lookup. Migration targets: route OAuth client credentials through resolve_secret or DB-backed credential authority; pass auth_shape to OAuth refresh callers; reduce .env/broad env propagation in execution_backends; keep provider_execution_registry metadata-only; preserve memory ingest as redaction-only with broader token patterns.
decisions.title: Secret-path inventory identifies keychain-backed and non-keychain credential reads

decisions.rationale: Mobile v1 conflated two incompatible use cases (external-user access and operator god-mode). Neither shipped, and the WebAuthn+per-command-approval ceremony was the wrong primitive for solo god-mode. Archived (not deleted): git branch archive/mobile-v1-2026-04-24 preserves every file at HEAD ec8aa0bf; revival doc is docs/archive/mobile-v1.md. Public branch commit removes ~1,200 LoC across runtime/capability/{sessions,approval_lifecycle}, runtime/webauthn/, runtime/mobile_security, 6 mobile API endpoints, 2 /workflows agent_sessions endpoints, and mobile-specific tests. Migration 220_archive_mobile_v1.sql drops the 6 mobile-only tables (device_enrollments, approval_requests, webauthn_challenges, mobile_bootstrap_tokens, mobile_sessions, mobile_session_budget_events). KEPT: capability_grants table (used by non-mobile workflow admission audit trail in storage/postgres/admission.py), gate_evaluations.grant_id and plan_envelope_hash columns (used by non-mobile submission_policy.py), and runtime/capability/{plan_envelope,resolver}.py (used by non-mobile control_commands.py). Operator god-mode will be solved via Tailscale-gated access to existing Moon at narrow viewport — no new Praxis code needed. External-user mobile is deferred until real external users exist and will be designed against that user, not in abstract.
decisions.title: Mobile v1 archived from public surface

decisions.rationale: The operator console (god-mode chat dispatcher) is bound by 8 anti-patterns derived from the mobile v1 archive. Full doc: docs/architecture/operator_console.md. Summary: (1) do not conflate solo-operator with multi-user, (2) no WebAuthn RP-ID ceremony for one principal on trusted LAN (Tailscale is the auth primitive), (3) no approval-request lifecycle for self-approved commands, (4) no bootstrap-token exchange — pre-seed one operator record, (5) no new mobile app surface — a responsive web page served by existing API, (6) no public-internet auth when tunnel-gated LAN works, (7) honor CLI-only Anthropic (routes through claude binary, not api.anthropic.com — respects decision.2026-04-20.anthropic-cli-only-restored), (8) no new workflow-launch endpoints — call canonical submission surface directly. Implementation phases: B.0 durable records (this decision + doc), B.1 normalized permission matrix (ProviderCLIProfile.normalized_permission_modes mapping 5 common modes to each CLIs native flags, agent_sessions API gains permission_mode param, no UI), B.2 chat UI at surfaces/app/operator-console/, B.3 plan rendering + approval gate, B.4 Tailscale VPS networking. Persistence uses existing interactive_agent_session authority, no new tables. Console gated on PRAXIS_OPERATOR_DEV_MODE=1.
decisions.title: Operator console anti-patterns from mobile v1 post-mortem

decisions.rationale: The compile task encompasses one fan-out + fork pipeline with three internal stages (plan_synthesis, plan_fork_author, plan_section_author). All four stages run on the same model — Together DeepSeek V4-Pro via API — for consistency across the pipeline. The model_access_control_matrix is the ON/OFF authority; turning ON together+DeepSeek-V4-Pro for all four stages lets the matrix-gated router select V4-Pro without rank-based pinning. V3.2 stays OFF.
decisions.title: Compile pipeline (compile + plan_synthesis + plan_fork_author + plan_section_author) all use Together DeepSeek V4-Pro

decisions.rationale: The effective provider control plane must be a DB-backed CQRS projection, not request-time assembly. Runtime/UI/agent consumers need one stable read model per runtime profile/provider/job/model with freshness metadata, explicit availability, and durable source refs so provider capability cannot be resurrected by helper code or hidden defaults.
decisions.title: Provider control plane is a projected read model

decisions.rationale: Provider/model disappearance must be explained by queryable control-plane reason codes, not inferred from scattered filters. Disabled catalog rows, lane-policy denial, missing transport admission, missing credentials, open circuits, provider usage failure, budget pressure, and runtime-profile non-admission all belong in the effective provider control plane so future agents can see exactly why capability is unavailable.
decisions.title: Provider removal reasons are first-class control-plane data

decisions.rationale: Strategic frame emerged 2026-04-26 sharpening the thesis past prior framings (agent platform, AI coworker, moment-in-time MDM). The kernel: Praxis is the system of record for AI-mediated work — the decisions, the workflows that produce them, and the assets that learn from both. Salesforce is system of record for customers, Workday for people; nothing is system of record for AI work. Praxis already has the bones: operation_catalog_registry + authority_events + receipts + cartridge_revisions concept + data_dictionary + data_pills + semantic_predicate_catalog + primitive_catalog + one-graph-many-lenses. Reframing them as system-of-record-for-AI-work makes the rest fall out. Positioning: SaaS for the work no SaaS sells. The dark-matter workflows (60-70% of company operations) that today live in spreadsheets, Slack, and senior people heads — too custom for SaaS, too judgment-heavy for pre-AI automation. AI compresses build cost ~50x. Praxis is the substrate that makes that buildable, ownable, auditable, learnable. The deliverable is a customer-owned cartridge that runs against a documented runtime contract (when Praxis leaves, the workflow still works). The moat is a compounding library of company-specific primitives extracted from real runs. The brutal risks: long-tail unit economics (must compress ~50x not 5x), cartridge portability (must not require Praxis runtime to run), authority cold-start (no history = no signal), library compounding (needs hundred-runs scale), workflow-as-authority write-back (must reconcile with downstream). Falsification: 14-day tests on authority resolver, cartridge emit, run-to-primitive extractor, customer-priced cartridge. 3 of 4 must pass.
decisions.title: Praxis is the system of record for AI-mediated work
